questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with a 30-pack-year smoking history and poorly controlled hypertension undergoes coronary catheterization after presenting with an acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction. Angiography reveals a ruptured plaque in the left anterior descending artery with an overlying thrombus. A biopsy of the plaque wall shows lipid-laden cells with a bubbly, vacuolated cytoplasm. Which of the following best describes the origin of these cells?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Monocytes that migrated into the intima and phagocytosed oxidized lipids

choices.label: B
choices.text: Smooth muscle cells that proliferated in response to platelet-derived growth factor

choices.label: C
choices.text: Endothelial cells that underwent apoptosis after exposure to reactive oxygen species

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fibroblasts that synthesized collagen to form the fibrous cap of the plaque

choices.label: E
choices.text: T lymphocytes that accumulated within the fatty streak during chronic inflammation

explanation.summary: Foam cells are lipid-laden macrophages formed when monocytes migrate into the intima, differentiate into macrophages, and phagocytose oxidized LDL.
explanation.whyCorrect: The bubbly, vacuolated cells described are foam cells. In atherosclerosis, endothelial damage increases permeability, allowing lipids to accumulate in the intima where they become oxidized. Monocytes recruited by cytokines enter the intima, differentiate into macrophages, and engulf the oxidized lipids, becoming foam cells with a characteristic bubbly appearance on histology.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Smooth muscle cells are recruited later in atheroma formation to cap the fatty streak and do not form the characteristic lipid-laden foam cells seen on histology.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Endothelial cells are damaged in the initial step of atherosclerosis but do not become the lipid-laden foam cells; endothelial injury triggers the inflammatory cascade that leads to foam cell formation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fibroblasts contribute to connective tissue in the fibrous cap but are not the lipid-laden cells with bubbly cytoplasm described in the question.
whyWrongByChoice.E: T lymphocytes are present in fatty streaks and contribute to inflammation, but the characteristic lipid-laden cells with vacuolated cytoplasm are macrophage-derived foam cells, not lymphocytes.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes and hyperlipidemia presents to her primary care physician for a routine visit. She has no current complaints. Her physician counsels her about atherosclerosis risk. The physician explains that atherosclerosis begins much earlier in life than most patients realize. At what age does early atherosclerosis typically begin to develop?

choices.label: A
choices.text: In utero during the third trimester of fetal development

choices.label: B
choices.text: During childhood and adolescence, typically between ages 15 and 35 years

choices.label: C
choices.text: During early adulthood, typically between ages 35 and 45 years

choices.label: D
choices.text: During middle age, typically between ages 45 and 55 years

choices.label: E
choices.text: During late adulthood, typically after age 65 years

explanation.summary: Early atherosclerosis is present in nearly all people ages 15 to 35 years in the United States, beginning silently during childhood.
explanation.whyCorrect: Although atherosclerosis is often thought of as a disease of older age, the pathologic process actually begins silently during childhood. In the United States, early atherosclerosis is present in nearly all people ages 15 to 35 years, well before clinical symptoms manifest.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Atherosclerosis does not begin in utero; although fetal endothelial cells are forming, the pathologic process of atheroma formation requires postnatal risk factor exposure and begins during childhood.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While atherosclerosis may become more clinically evident in early adulthood, the process begins much earlier, during childhood and adolescence.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Middle age is when atherosclerosis typically becomes clinically evident with symptoms, but the pathologic process has been developing silently for decades by that point.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Late adulthood is associated with advanced atherosclerotic disease and complications, but the underlying process began decades earlier during childhood.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a history of smoking and hypertension dies suddenly while exercising. At autopsy, the pathologist identifies a large thrombus overlying a ruptured atherosclerotic plaque in a major coronary artery. The coronary arteries otherwise show only mild-to-moderate atherosclerosis. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which plaque rupture led to thrombus formation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Release of tissue plasminogen activator from damaged endothelial cells activated the fibrinolytic cascade

choices.label: B
choices.text: Exposure of vascular smooth muscle triggered complement-mediated lysis of circulating erythrocytes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Exposure of subendothelial collagen to circulating platelets and clotting proteins initiated the coagulation cascade

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rupture of the plaque released cholesterol crystals that directly activated Factor XII in the intrinsic pathway

choices.label: E
choices.text: Disruption of laminar blood flow caused stasis and activation of the protein C anticoagulant pathway

explanation.summary: Plaque rupture exposes subendothelial collagen to platelets and clotting proteins, initiating thrombus formation that can rapidly occlude the artery.
explanation.whyCorrect: When the thin fibrous cap of an atherosclerotic plaque ruptures, the underlying endothelial collagen is exposed to circulating platelets and clotting proteins in the blood. This initiates platelet aggregation and thrombus formation, which can cause rapid arterial occlusion leading to myocardial infarction and sudden death.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Tissue plasminogen activator promotes fibrinolysis (clot breakdown), not clot formation; its release would counteract rather than promote thrombosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Complement-mediated erythrocyte lysis is not the mechanism of thrombus formation after plaque rupture; the key event is platelet activation upon collagen exposure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While cholesterol crystals can embolize from ruptured plaques, the primary mechanism of thrombus formation is exposure of subendothelial collagen to platelets and clotting factors, not direct Factor XII activation by cholesterol.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Protein C is an anticoagulant pathway that inhibits clotting; disruption of laminar flow contributes to endothelial damage but does not activate an anticoagulant pathway to form thrombi.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a history of diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia presents with cramping pain in both calves that occurs after walking two blocks and resolves with rest. Physical examination reveals diminished pedal pulses bilaterally. Which of the following best describes the pathologic process causing this patient's symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Venous insufficiency causing blood pooling and tissue edema in the lower extremities

choices.label: B
choices.text: Autoimmune vasculitis with granulomatous inflammation of medium-sized arteries

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vasospasm of peripheral arteries triggered by cold exposure and sympathetic activation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Deep venous thrombosis with retrograde venous flow and valvular incompetence

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atherosclerotic narrowing of peripheral arteries causing tissue ischemia during exertion

explanation.summary: Intermittent claudication is exertional leg pain caused by atherosclerotic narrowing of peripheral arteries that limits blood flow during activity.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient presents with classic intermittent claudication—cramping leg pain with exertion that resolves with rest—a hallmark of peripheral artery disease (PAD). Atherosclerosis in the peripheral arteries, especially the legs, narrows the arterial lumen, restricting blood flow. During exertion, the increased oxygen demand cannot be met, producing ischemic pain that resolves when demand decreases at rest.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Venous insufficiency causes edema, skin changes, and aching that worsens with standing, not the exertional cramping pain with diminished pulses seen in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Autoimmune vasculitis (such as polyarteritis nodosa) can affect medium arteries but typically presents with systemic symptoms, skin lesions, and organ involvement rather than classic intermittent claudication.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Vasospasm of peripheral arteries (as in Raynaud phenomenon) typically affects the digits and is triggered by cold, not exertion, and presents with color changes rather than calf cramping.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Deep venous thrombosis presents with unilateral leg swelling, warmth, and pain, not bilateral exertional cramping relieved by rest with diminished arterial pulses.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with known atherosclerotic disease undergoes Doppler ultrasonography of the lower extremities, which reveals significant stenosis of the right femoral artery. His physician prescribes a statin medication. In addition to lowering LDL cholesterol, which of the following is another beneficial effect of statin therapy in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct thrombolysis of existing arterial thrombi overlying atherosclerotic plaques

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inhibition of vascular smooth muscle proliferation in the tunica media of affected arteries

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reversal of existing arterial calcification within established atherosclerotic plaques

choices.label: D
choices.text: Plaque stabilization and decreased endothelial cell dysfunction in affected arteries

choices.label: E
choices.text: Direct vasodilation of stenotic arterial segments to immediately restore blood flow

explanation.summary: In addition to reducing circulating cholesterol and LDL-C levels, statins provide plaque stabilization and decrease endothelial cell dysfunction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Statins have pleiotropic effects beyond cholesterol reduction. They stabilize existing atherosclerotic plaques, making them less prone to rupture, and decrease endothelial cell dysfunction. These additional benefits contribute to reduced cardiovascular events.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Statins do not have thrombolytic activity; thrombolysis requires specific fibrinolytic agents such as tissue plasminogen activator (tPA).
whyWrongByChoice.B: While statins may have some anti-proliferative effects, their primary recognized benefits beyond lipid lowering are plaque stabilization and improvement of endothelial function, not direct inhibition of smooth muscle proliferation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Statins do not reverse existing arterial calcification; regression of atherosclerosis takes 12-24 months and involves slowing progression rather than reversing calcification.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Statins do not cause direct vasodilation; medications like nitrates and calcium channel blockers provide vasodilatory effects.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman presents to her cardiologist for evaluation of newly diagnosed coronary artery disease. She has no prior history of heart disease and is surprised by the diagnosis. Her physician explains that her risk increased significantly after menopause. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which menopause increases atherosclerosis risk?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Estrogen deficiency leads to unfavorable cholesterol changes, increased arterial stiffness, and endothelial dysfunction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Loss of estrogen leads to increased platelet aggregation and accelerated thrombus formation on intact endothelium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased progesterone levels after menopause directly promote foam cell formation in the coronary intima

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased follicle-stimulating hormone after menopause reduces hepatic clearance of circulating low-density lipoproteins

choices.label: E
choices.text: Loss of estrogen causes direct smooth muscle cell apoptosis in the coronary arterial wall leading to plaque instability

explanation.summary: Menopause increases atherosclerosis risk through estrogen deficiency, which causes unfavorable cholesterol changes, increased arterial stiffness, and endothelial dysfunction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Estrogen has protective cardiovascular effects. After menopause, estrogen deficiency leads to unfavorable changes in cholesterol levels (increased LDL, decreased HDL), increased arterial stiffness, and endothelial dysfunction—all of which accelerate atherosclerosis development.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Progesterone levels decrease, not increase, after menopause; progesterone does not directly promote foam cell formation in the mechanism described for postmenopausal atherosclerosis risk.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While estrogen loss affects cardiovascular health, the primary mechanism is through cholesterol changes, arterial stiffness, and endothelial dysfunction, not direct promotion of platelet aggregation on intact endothelium.
whyWrongByChoice.D: FSH actually increases after menopause, not decreases; the mechanism of increased atherosclerosis risk is related to estrogen deficiency, not changes in FSH affecting hepatic lipoprotein clearance.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Estrogen deficiency does not cause direct smooth muscle cell apoptosis in coronary arteries; the mechanism involves systemic metabolic and vascular changes rather than direct cellular death.

questions.stem: A pathologist examines a section of the aorta from a 45-year-old man who died in a motor vehicle accident. Histologic examination reveals clusters of lipid-laden cells beneath the endothelium, along with scattered T cells and smooth muscle cells. No fibrous cap or calcification is present. Which of the following stages of atherosclerotic plaque development is best represented by these findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Endothelial injury response with monocyte recruitment and adhesion molecule expression

choices.label: B
choices.text: Foam cell formation with initial lipid accumulation beneath the endothelium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Smooth muscle recruitment with formation of a thin fibrous cap over the lesion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fatty streak formation with clustered foam cells, T cells, and smooth muscle cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Transformation to atherosclerotic plaque with calcification and luminal narrowing

explanation.summary: Fatty streaks are the first visible atherosclerotic lesions on microscopy, composed of clustered foam cells along with T cells, platelets, and smooth muscle cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: The histologic findings describe a fatty streak—clusters of lipid-laden foam cells with T cells and smooth muscle cells, without a fibrous cap or calcification. Fatty streaks are formed when foam cells accumulate and cluster, representing the first visible atherosclerotic regions on microscopy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The endothelial injury response involves cytokine release and monocyte recruitment but does not yet show the clustered lipid-laden cells, T cells, and smooth muscle cells described in this specimen.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Foam cell formation involves individual macrophages engulfing oxidized lipids, but the clustering of these cells with T cells and smooth muscle cells indicates progression to the fatty streak stage.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Smooth muscle recruitment with fibrous cap formation occurs after the fatty streak stage; this specimen lacks a fibrous cap, placing it at an earlier stage.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Transformation to an atherosclerotic plaque involves calcification, which is absent in this specimen, indicating the lesion has not yet progressed to this advanced stage.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with a history of smoking and hyperlipidemia presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of left-sided weakness and slurred speech. CT angiography of the head and neck reveals a 90% stenosis of the right internal carotid artery. Which of the following is the most likely source of this patient's neurological symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Embolization of cholesterol crystals from a ruptured atherosclerotic plaque to cerebral vessels

choices.label: B
choices.text: Vasospasm of the middle cerebral artery caused by sympathetic nervous system hyperactivation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hemorrhagic transformation of a pre-existing lacunar infarct in the basal ganglia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Venous sinus thrombosis caused by hypercoagulability from chronic inflammatory state

choices.label: E
choices.text: Dissection of the vertebral artery with subsequent posterior circulation ischemia

explanation.summary: Atheroemboli from ruptured plaques can release cholesterol crystals that embolize to smaller cerebral arteries, causing stroke.
explanation.whyCorrect: In the setting of severe carotid atherosclerosis, plaque rupture can release cholesterol crystals and thrombus fragments that embolize downstream to cerebral vessels, causing ischemic stroke. This is a well-recognized complication of carotid atherosclerosis and explains the sudden-onset focal neurological deficits.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Vasospasm of cerebral arteries is typically associated with subarachnoid hemorrhage, not atherosclerotic carotid disease; the 90% carotid stenosis points to an atheroembolic source.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hemorrhagic transformation of a lacunar infarct would not be the primary event in a patient with severe carotid stenosis presenting with acute neurological deficits consistent with anterior circulation ischemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Venous sinus thrombosis presents with headache, papilledema, and seizures rather than the acute focal deficits seen with arterial ischemia from carotid atherosclerosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vertebral artery dissection affects the posterior circulation; this patient's symptoms (left-sided weakness, slurred speech) with right carotid stenosis are more consistent with anterior circulation atheroembolism.

questions.stem: A researcher is studying the early steps of atheroma formation in a mouse model. Endothelial cells in the aorta of the experimental mice show increased expression of vascular cell adhesion molecules (VCAMs) and release of pro-inflammatory cytokines. Which of the following is the primary purpose of VCAM expression in the pathogenesis of atherosclerosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To promote smooth muscle cell migration from the tunica media to the intima for fibrous cap formation

choices.label: B
choices.text: To facilitate oxidation of low-density lipoprotein particles within the subendothelial space

choices.label: C
choices.text: To increase endothelial permeability allowing red blood cell extravasation into the vessel wall

choices.label: D
choices.text: To stimulate platelet degranulation and initiate the extrinsic coagulation cascade

choices.label: E
choices.text: To enable monocyte adhesion to and infiltration of the damaged endothelium

explanation.summary: VCAMs are adhesion molecules produced by endothelial cells that allow monocytes to adhere to and infiltrate the endothelium during the inflammatory response to injury.
explanation.whyCorrect: In response to endothelial injury, endothelial cells release cytokines to recruit monocytes and produce adhesion molecules such as VCAMs. These adhesion molecules allow monocytes to adhere to the endothelial surface and then infiltrate through the endothelium as part of the inflammatory reparative process—a critical early step in atheroma formation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Smooth muscle cell migration occurs later in atheroma formation (Step 5) and is driven by growth factors, not by VCAM expression on endothelial cells.
whyWrongByChoice.B: LDL oxidation occurs in the subendothelial space after lipids penetrate through the damaged endothelium; VCAMs facilitate immune cell adhesion rather than lipid oxidation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While endothelial damage does increase permeability, VCAMs specifically facilitate leukocyte (particularly monocyte) adhesion, not red blood cell extravasation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Platelet degranulation and coagulation cascade activation occur during thrombus formation after plaque rupture, which is a later complication; VCAMs are involved in early monocyte recruitment.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with a history of hypertension and smoking presents to the emergency department with severe abdominal pain. Physical examination reveals a pulsatile abdominal mass. CT angiography shows a 7-cm infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm with signs of contained rupture. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiologic mechanism by which atherosclerosis contributed to aneurysm formation at this location?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chronic inflammation from atherosclerosis caused fibrinoid necrosis and transmural vessel wall destruction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atherosclerotic plaques caused turbulent blood flow that weakened and dilated the vessel wall

choices.label: C
choices.text: Atherosclerotic plaques directly eroded through the tunica media causing a pseudoaneurysm

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cholesterol crystal embolization from upstream plaques obstructed the vasa vasorum of the aorta

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atherosclerosis-induced endothelial dysfunction allowed blood to dissect between the intima and media

explanation.summary: Atheromas protrude into the vessel lumen, converting normal laminar flow to turbulent flow that injures, weakens, and dilates the vessel wall, leading to aneurysm formation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Atherosclerotic plaques protrude into the vessel lumen, causing normal laminar blood flow to become turbulent. This turbulent flow injures the vessel wall, which can weaken and dilate over time, forming an aneurysm. Aneurysms most often occur at arterial branch points, including the abdominal aorta.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Fibrinoid necrosis is characteristic of malignant hypertension and certain vasculitides, not the typical mechanism of atherosclerosis-related aneurysm formation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Atherosclerotic plaques form in the intima and do not directly erode through the media to form pseudoaneurysms; the mechanism involves turbulent flow weakening the entire wall.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While cholesterol crystals can embolize from ruptured plaques, obstruction of the vasa vasorum is not the primary mechanism of atherosclerosis-related aortic aneurysm formation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aortic dissection involves blood tracking between vessel wall layers and is a distinct pathology from aneurysm formation; atherosclerosis-related aneurysms develop from wall weakening due to turbulent flow.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with hypercholesterolemia and a strong family history of premature coronary artery disease asks his physician about the most important risk factor for developing atherosclerosis. The physician explains that while modifiable risk factors are important to address, one non-modifiable factor stands out as the most significant independent predictor. Which of the following is the most important independent risk factor for atherosclerosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypertension requiring multi-drug antihypertensive therapy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Male sex with testosterone-mediated endothelial dysfunction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diabetes mellitus with chronic hyperglycemia and insulin resistance

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increasing age with progressive accumulation of atherosclerotic plaques

choices.label: E
choices.text: Family history of premature atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease

explanation.summary: Family history (genetics) is the most important independent risk factor for atherosclerosis among the non-modifiable constitutional risk factors.
explanation.whyCorrect: Family history is identified as the most important independent risk factor for atherosclerosis. Genetic predisposition plays a major role in determining an individual's susceptibility to atherosclerotic disease, independent of modifiable risk factors.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hypertension is a major modifiable risk factor for atherosclerosis but is not the most important independent risk factor; it can be addressed with lifestyle changes and medications.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Male sex is a non-modifiable risk factor, but family history is considered the most important independent risk factor; additionally, the sex-based risk difference diminishes after women reach menopause.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Diabetes mellitus is a major modifiable risk factor but is not classified as the most important independent risk factor for atherosclerosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Increasing age is a non-modifiable risk factor that reflects the progressive nature of atherosclerotic plaque development, but family history is considered the most important independent risk factor.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with peripheral artery disease undergoes conventional arterial angiography of the right lower extremity. The angiogram reveals multiple areas of luminal narrowing in the right iliac and femoral arteries. Her physician recommends initiating statin therapy. After how many months can regression of atherosclerosis typically be expected with statin treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1 to 3 months after initiation of statin therapy

choices.label: B
choices.text: 3 to 6 months after initiation of statin therapy

choices.label: C
choices.text: 12 to 24 months after initiation of statin therapy

choices.label: D
choices.text: 6 to 9 months after initiation of statin therapy

choices.label: E
choices.text: 36 to 48 months after initiation of statin therapy

explanation.summary: The regression of atherosclerosis with statin therapy typically takes 12 to 24 months.
explanation.whyCorrect: Statins decrease circulating cholesterol and LDL-C levels, slow progression of cholesterol deposition, provide plaque stabilization, and decrease endothelial dysfunction. However, actual regression of atherosclerosis is a slow process that typically requires 12 to 24 months of statin therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: One to three months is too early to expect regression of atherosclerosis; while cholesterol levels may improve quickly, structural changes in plaques take much longer.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Three to six months is insufficient time for atherosclerosis regression, although beneficial effects on lipid levels and some plaque stabilization may begin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Six to nine months is still earlier than the typical timeframe for atherosclerosis regression, which requires 12 to 24 months.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thirty-six to 48 months overestimates the time needed; regression can typically be observed within 12 to 24 months of statin therapy.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man with coronary artery disease undergoes coronary artery biopsy during a catheterization procedure. Histologic examination reveals cholesterol clefts within the intimal layer of the vessel. Which of the following best describes the process that leads to cholesterol cleft formation within an atherosclerotic lesion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Precipitation of circulating LDL cholesterol directly into the subendothelial space through damaged endothelium

choices.label: B
choices.text: Apoptosis of lipid-laden foam cells leaving behind residual lipid deposits that fill the spaces of dead cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Active secretion of cholesterol esters by smooth muscle cells recruited to form the fibrous cap

choices.label: D
choices.text: Calcium-mediated transformation of fatty streaks into calcified atheromatous plaques

choices.label: E
choices.text: Endothelial cell necrosis releasing intracellular cholesterol stores into the vessel wall matrix

explanation.summary: Cholesterol clefts form when foam cells undergo apoptosis and the lipids they contained remain behind, filling the spaces left by the dead cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: During fatty streak formation, foam cells (lipid-laden macrophages) eventually undergo apoptosis. The lipids they had phagocytosed remain behind, filling the spaces formed by the apoptotic cells. These residual lipid deposits are known as cholesterol clefts and may appear in the intima or tunica media.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While lipids do enter through damaged endothelium, cholesterol clefts specifically result from foam cell apoptosis leaving residual lipids, not from direct precipitation of circulating LDL.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Smooth muscle cells are recruited to form the fibrous cap but do not actively secrete cholesterol esters to form cholesterol clefts; the clefts derive from apoptotic foam cells.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Calcification is a later step in atherosclerotic plaque transformation and is distinct from cholesterol cleft formation, which occurs during the fatty streak stage.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Endothelial cells are damaged in the early steps of atherosclerosis, but cholesterol clefts arise from apoptotic foam cells (macrophage-derived), not from endothelial cell necrosis.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman with a history of rheumatoid arthritis, subclinical hypothyroidism, and gout presents for cardiovascular risk assessment. She does not smoke, has normal blood pressure, and has well-controlled blood glucose. Her physician explains that some of her medical conditions may contribute to atherosclerosis risk. Which of the following best categorizes these conditions in terms of atherosclerosis risk?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are major modifiable risk factors equivalent to hypertension and diabetes

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are non-modifiable constitutional risk factors similar to age and sex

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are disorders that may serve as minor risk factors for atherosclerosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are protective factors that decrease the progression of atherosclerotic disease

choices.label: E
choices.text: They have no established association with atherosclerosis development or progression

explanation.summary: Rheumatoid arthritis, subclinical hypothyroidism, and gout are classified as disorders that may be minor risk factors for atherosclerosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Rheumatoid arthritis, gout, and subclinical hypothyroidism (or hyperthyroidism) are listed among disorders that may serve as risk factors for atherosclerosis. They are classified separately from the major modifiable risk factors (smoking, hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidemia, obesity) and the non-modifiable constitutional factors (genetics, age, sex).

whyWrongByChoice.A: These conditions are not classified as major modifiable risk factors; major modifiable risk factors include hypertension, diabetes, smoking, dyslipidemia, and abdominal obesity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: These are not non-modifiable constitutional risk factors; constitutional risk factors include genetics/family history, increasing age, and male gender.
whyWrongByChoice.D: These conditions are associated with increased, not decreased, atherosclerosis risk; chronic inflammation and metabolic derangements from these conditions may contribute to accelerated atherogenesis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: These conditions do have established associations with atherosclerosis; they are specifically listed as disorders that may serve as risk factors.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old man with a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia presents with squeezing chest pain that has occurred over the past 4 months exclusively during his morning jogs. The pain radiates to his left arm and consistently resolves within 3 minutes of stopping exercise. His resting ECG is normal. A sublingual nitroglycerin tablet relieves the pain when taken during an episode. Which of the following best describes the underlying pathophysiology of this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rupture of an atherosclerotic plaque with superimposed thrombus formation in a coronary artery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intermittent coronary artery spasm with transient reduction in myocardial oxygen supply

choices.label: C
choices.text: Microvascular dysfunction with impaired coronary flow reserve despite normal epicardial arteries

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fixed atherosclerotic narrowing causing myocardial oxygen demand to exceed supply during exertion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aortic valve stenosis with decreased coronary perfusion pressure during systolic ejection

explanation.summary: Stable angina results from fixed atherosclerotic narrowing of coronary arteries that limits blood flow; during exertion, myocardial oxygen demand exceeds the restricted supply.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient has classic stable angina: predictable exertional chest pain relieved by rest and nitroglycerin, with a normal resting ECG. Stable angina is caused by atherosclerotic narrowing of coronary arteries that restricts blood flow. At rest, the reduced flow is adequate. During exertion, myocardial oxygen demand increases beyond what the diseased vessel can supply, causing ischemia and chest pain.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Plaque rupture with superimposed thrombus characterizes unstable angina or myocardial infarction, which presents with unpredictable chest pain at rest, not the predictable exertional pattern described.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Intermittent coronary artery spasm describes vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina, which occurs at rest and is not related to exertion; this patient's symptoms are predictably exercise-induced.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Microvascular dysfunction can cause angina-like symptoms, but this patient's classic presentation with exertional pain, rest relief, and nitroglycerin response is most consistent with epicardial atherosclerotic disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aortic valve stenosis can cause exertional chest pain, but the clinical scenario describes classic stable angina without murmur findings or other features suggesting valvular disease.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe substernal chest pain that began 30 minutes ago while she was watching television. She has a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hyperlipidemia. The pain has not improved with rest. Her ECG shows ST-segment depressions in leads V3-V6. Serum troponins measured with a traditional assay are negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Admit to the hospital for monitoring and treatment as for acute coronary syndrome

choices.label: B
choices.text: Discharge with sublingual nitroglycerin prescription and outpatient cardiology follow-up

choices.label: C
choices.text: Schedule outpatient exercise stress testing within the next 2 weeks

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prescribe daily aspirin and atorvastatin with dietary counseling and discharge home

choices.label: E
choices.text: Perform immediate emergent coronary artery bypass grafting surgery

explanation.summary: This patient has unstable angina with rest pain, ST depressions, and negative troponins, requiring hospital admission and treatment as for acute coronary syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient presents with new-onset chest pain at rest lasting more than 10 minutes, with ST-segment depressions on ECG—classic features of unstable angina or NSTEMI. Even with negative traditional troponin assays, this presentation requires in-patient admission for monitoring and treatment as for acute coronary syndrome, including antiplatelet agents, heparin, beta-blockers, and coronary angiography within 24 hours.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Discharging this patient would be dangerous; rest pain with ECG changes indicates unstable angina, which carries high mortality risk and requires in-patient management.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Outpatient stress testing is inappropriate for a patient with active chest pain and ECG changes suggesting acute coronary syndrome; exercise stress testing is contraindicated in ongoing unstable angina.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While aspirin and statin are components of treatment, simply prescribing them and discharging would be inadequate for a patient presenting with unstable angina requiring emergent evaluation and monitoring.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Emergent coronary artery bypass grafting is not the initial step; coronary angiography should first be performed to determine the extent and location of disease, and CABG is reserved for specific indications like left main or multivessel disease.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a 20-pack-year smoking history and no other cardiovascular risk factors presents with recurrent episodes of chest pain that occur while he is asleep, typically between 2 and 4 AM. The pain resolves within minutes after taking sublingual nitroglycerin. A resting ECG obtained between episodes is normal. His physician suspects vasospastic angina. Which of the following diagnostic findings would be expected during an acute episode?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ST-segment elevations or depressions on the electrocardiogram during the pain episode

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pathological Q waves in the anterior leads indicating transmural myocardial necrosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Markedly elevated high-sensitivity troponin levels with normal creatine kinase-MB levels

choices.label: D
choices.text: New-onset left bundle branch block pattern with widened QRS complex duration

choices.label: E
choices.text: Diffuse ST-segment elevations in all leads with PR-segment depressions suggesting pericarditis

explanation.summary: During vasospastic angina episodes, the ECG shows ST-segment elevations or depressions; the ECG is normal between episodes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina shows a normal ECG between pain episodes. During pain, there will be ST elevations or depressions reflecting transient myocardial ischemia caused by coronary artery spasm. This ECG pattern normalizes once the spasm resolves.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Cardiac enzyme levels (including troponins) are normal in vasospastic angina because there is no myocardial necrosis—the ischemia is transient and reversible with spasm resolution.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pathological Q waves indicate completed transmural myocardial infarction with irreversible necrosis, not the transient ischemia of vasospastic angina.
whyWrongByChoice.D: New left bundle branch block suggests acute myocardial infarction or structural heart disease, not the transient ST changes seen during vasospastic angina episodes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Diffuse ST elevations with PR depressions suggest pericarditis, which has a different pathophysiology; vasospastic angina shows focal ST changes corresponding to the territory of the spasming coronary artery.

questions.stem: A 59-year-old man with stable angina is prescribed sublingual nitroglycerin for acute symptom relief. After taking his first dose during an episode of chest pain, he notices the pain resolves but he develops a throbbing headache. He asks his physician why this side effect occurs. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which nitroglycerin causes headache?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nitroglycerin causes reflex tachycardia that increases intracranial arterial pulsation pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nitroglycerin produces cyclic GMP-mediated vasodilation that affects cerebral and meningeal vessels

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nitroglycerin inhibits prostaglandin synthesis leading to impaired cerebrovascular autoregulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nitroglycerin blocks calcium channels in cerebral smooth muscle causing excessive vasodilation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Nitroglycerin releases histamine from mast cells in the meninges triggering neurogenic inflammation

explanation.summary: Nitroglycerin produces cyclic GMP that relaxes vascular smooth muscle, causing vasodilation; this vasodilation affects cerebral and meningeal vessels, producing headache in over 60% of patients.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nitroglycerin works by producing cyclic guanosine monophosphate (cGMP) that relaxes vascular smooth muscles, causing vasodilation. While this vasodilation is beneficial for reducing cardiac preload and relieving angina, it also dilates cerebral and meningeal blood vessels, which produces headache—the most common adverse effect, occurring in more than 60% of patients.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While nitroglycerin can cause reflex tachycardia, the headache is primarily due to direct cGMP-mediated vasodilation of cerebral and meningeal vessels, not reflex-mediated changes in intracranial pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nitroglycerin does not inhibit prostaglandin synthesis; it works through the nitric oxide-cGMP pathway to produce vasodilation, which is the mechanism behind both its therapeutic effect and headache side effect.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nitroglycerin does not block calcium channels; calcium channel blockers are a separate drug class. Nitroglycerin produces vasodilation through the cGMP pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nitroglycerin does not release histamine from meningeal mast cells; the headache mechanism is direct cGMP-mediated vasodilation of cerebral vessels.

questions.stem: A 71-year-old woman with stable angina refractory to medical therapy undergoes coronary angiography, which reveals severe stenosis of the left main coronary artery. Her cardiologist recommends coronary artery bypass grafting rather than percutaneous coronary intervention with stenting. Which of the following best explains why bypass surgery is preferred over stenting in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stenting is contraindicated in female patients over age 70 due to increased risk of in-stent thrombosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stent occlusion in the left main artery would deprive the entire left coronary circulation of blood flow

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bypass grafting has a lower procedural mortality rate than stenting regardless of lesion location

choices.label: D
choices.text: Left main stenosis is always caused by vasospasm rather than atherosclerosis and does not respond to stenting

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stents cannot be deployed in arteries larger than 4 millimeters in diameter due to size limitations

explanation.summary: The left main coronary artery gives rise to both the circumflex and left anterior descending arteries, so stent occlusion in this location would deprive the entire left coronary circulation of blood.
explanation.whyCorrect: Coronary artery bypass surgery is preferred for left main coronary artery disease because that vessel gives rise to both the circumflex and left anterior descending arteries, supplying a large area of myocardium. Stenting in this area is problematic because if the stent were to occlude, it would deprive the entire left coronary circulation of blood, potentially causing a catastrophic myocardial infarction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Age and sex are not absolute contraindications to stenting; the preference for bypass in this case is based on the anatomic location of the lesion, not patient demographics.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bypass grafting does not universally have lower procedural mortality than stenting; the preference depends on the specific anatomy, with left main disease being a key indication for surgery.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Left main stenosis is caused by atherosclerosis, not vasospasm; the reason bypass is preferred is the catastrophic consequences of potential stent occlusion at this critical location.
whyWrongByChoice.E: There is no 4-mm size limitation for coronary stents; stents are available in various sizes and can be deployed in the left main artery, but the risk of occlusion at this critical location makes bypass surgery the preferred approach.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with stable angina is started on metoprolol. His physician explains that beta-blockers are the first-line treatment for his condition. The patient asks whether this medication will help him live longer. Which of the following is the most accurate response regarding the effect of beta-blockers on mortality in stable angina?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Beta-blockers significantly reduce all-cause mortality by preventing atherosclerotic plaque progression

choices.label: B
choices.text: Beta-blockers are the only drug class proven to reduce cardiovascular mortality in stable angina

choices.label: C
choices.text: Beta-blockers reduce mortality only when combined with calcium channel blocker therapy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Beta-blockers reduce angina frequency and severity but do not decrease the overall mortality rate

choices.label: E
choices.text: Beta-blockers reduce mortality by directly stabilizing atherosclerotic plaques in coronary arteries

explanation.summary: Beta-blockers are first-line treatment for stable angina and reduce angina frequency and severity, but they do not decrease the overall mortality rate.
explanation.whyCorrect: While beta-blockers are the first-line treatment for stable angina due to their effectiveness in reducing angina frequency and severity through decreased myocardial oxygen demand, they do not decrease the overall mortality rate. Statins, not beta-blockers, are the drug class that provides primary prevention and mortality reduction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Beta-blockers do not reduce mortality in stable angina and do not prevent atherosclerotic plaque progression; statins are the class that slows plaque progression and reduces mortality.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Combining beta-blockers with calcium channel blockers does not confer a mortality benefit; neither class alone or in combination has been shown to reduce mortality in stable angina.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Beta-blockers are not proven to reduce cardiovascular mortality in stable angina; statins (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors) are the drug class that reduces mortality and prevents myocardial infarction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Beta-blockers do not directly stabilize atherosclerotic plaques; plaque stabilization is an effect of statin therapy.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman presents to her physician with atypical chest discomfort that occurs intermittently with varying levels of exertion. Her physician determines she has an intermediate pre-test probability of coronary artery disease and orders an exercise stress test. During the test, the ECG shows 3-mm ST-segment depressions in leads V4-V6 at peak exercise, and the patient reports chest tightness. Which of the following additional findings would also constitute a positive stress test result?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Heart rate increase of 40 beats per minute above resting baseline during peak exercise

choices.label: B
choices.text: Systolic blood pressure increase of 30 mmHg above resting baseline during peak exercise

choices.label: C
choices.text: Achievement of 85% of maximum predicted heart rate without symptoms at peak exercise

choices.label: D
choices.text: Development of sinus tachycardia with a heart rate of 150 beats per minute at peak effort

choices.label: E
choices.text: Exercise-induced drop in systolic blood pressure of greater than 10 mmHg from baseline

explanation.summary: Exercise-induced hypotension (systolic blood pressure drop >10 mmHg) qualifies as a positive exercise stress test, in addition to ischemic ECG changes.
explanation.whyCorrect: An exercise stress test is positive if there are ischemic ECG changes during exercise (classically ST-segment depressions >2-3 mm) or if there is exercise-induced hypotension, defined as a drop in systolic blood pressure of more than 10 mmHg. Both findings indicate hemodynamically significant coronary artery disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: An appropriate heart rate increase during exercise is a normal physiologic response and does not constitute a positive stress test finding.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A systolic blood pressure increase of 30 mmHg during exercise is a normal hemodynamic response to exertion and is expected, not pathologic.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Achieving 85% of maximum predicted heart rate without symptoms indicates an adequate exercise workload and is actually a reassuring finding, not a positive test result.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sinus tachycardia during exercise is a normal physiologic response; a positive stress test requires ischemic ECG changes (ST depressions) or exercise-induced hypotension, not simply an elevated heart rate.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man with suspected stable angina is unable to walk on a treadmill due to severe osteoarthritis of both knees. His physician considers pharmacologic stress testing as an alternative. Which of the following agents is most appropriate for pharmacologic stress testing in this patient who also has a history of well-controlled asthma?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Adenosine, which is a peripheral vasodilator that increases coronary blood flow differentially

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dipyridamole, which is a peripheral vasodilator that inhibits adenosine reuptake and metabolism

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dobutamine, which has positive inotropic and chronotropic effects that increase cardiac work

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ergonovine, which induces coronary vasospasm to evaluate for Prinzmetal angina

choices.label: E
choices.text: Acetylcholine, which is used to provoke coronary vasospasm during angiographic evaluation

explanation.summary: Dobutamine is the appropriate pharmacologic stress agent for this patient because vasodilators (adenosine, dipyridamole) are contraindicated in patients with asthma.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pharmacologic stress testing can use either peripheral vasodilators (adenosine, dipyridamole) or positive inotropic/chronotropic agents (dobutamine). However, vasodilators are contraindicated in patients with severe asthma. Since this patient has asthma, dobutamine is the appropriate choice as it provides cardiac stress through positive inotropic and chronotropic effects without the bronchoconstriction risk.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adenosine is a vasodilator used for pharmacologic stress testing but is contraindicated in patients with severe asthma due to the risk of bronchoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Dipyridamole is another vasodilator option for stress testing but shares the same contraindication as adenosine in patients with severe asthma.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ergonovine is used to provoke coronary vasospasm during angiography for suspected vasospastic angina, not as a standard pharmacologic stress test agent for evaluating stable angina.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acetylcholine is used in a controlled angiography lab setting to provoke coronary vasospasm for diagnosing Prinzmetal angina, not for routine pharmacologic stress testing.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a history of cocaine use and heavy smoking presents to the emergency department at 3 AM with severe chest pain that woke him from sleep. The pain began suddenly and is described as crushing and substernal. His ECG shows transient ST-segment elevations in leads II, III, and aVF that resolve after administration of sublingual nitroglycerin. Coronary angiography performed the next day shows no significant atherosclerotic disease. Which of the following is the most appropriate long-term management for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Daily aspirin and high-dose atorvastatin with outpatient exercise stress testing in 4 weeks

choices.label: B
choices.text: Coronary artery bypass grafting with bilateral mammary artery grafts to prevent future events

choices.label: C
choices.text: Long-acting beta-blocker therapy with metoprolol succinate as first-line preventive treatment

choices.label: D
choices.text: Smoking cessation and calcium channel blocker therapy with diltiazem or amlodipine

choices.label: E
choices.text: Dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel for 12 months followed by aspirin alone

explanation.summary: This patient has vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina, best managed with smoking cessation and calcium channel blockers as first-line therapy.
explanation.whyCorrect: This presentation—rest angina at night in a smoker and cocaine user with transient ST elevations that resolve with nitroglycerin and no angiographic coronary disease—is classic for vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina. First-line management includes smoking cessation (and drug cessation) along with calcium channel blockers such as diltiazem or amlodipine.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While aspirin and statins are appropriate for atherosclerosis-related angina, this patient has vasospastic angina without significant atherosclerotic disease; calcium channel blockers and smoking cessation are the primary treatments.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Coronary artery bypass grafting is indicated for significant coronary artery disease (left main or multivessel disease), not vasospastic angina with normal coronary arteries.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta-blockers are first-line for stable angina but are not the primary treatment for vasospastic angina; some beta-blockers may worsen coronary vasospasm by allowing unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Dual antiplatelet therapy is used after acute coronary syndromes with atherosclerotic plaque rupture or after stent placement, not for vasospastic angina with normal coronary arteries.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with stable angina controlled on metoprolol and sublingual nitroglycerin is prescribed atorvastatin by his cardiologist. The patient asks why a cholesterol-lowering medication is necessary when his primary concern is chest pain. Which of the following best explains the rationale for statin therapy in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Statins directly relieve anginal chest pain by increasing coronary artery blood flow through vasodilation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Statins reduce LDL cholesterol levels and stabilize coronary artery plaques, offering primary prevention against myocardial infarction and reducing cardiac mortality

choices.label: C
choices.text: Statins enhance the antianginal effects of beta-blockers by synergistically reducing myocardial oxygen demand

choices.label: D
choices.text: Statins prevent coronary artery spasm by blocking calcium-dependent smooth muscle contraction in the vessel wall

choices.label: E
choices.text: Statins inhibit platelet aggregation at the site of atherosclerotic plaques, preventing acute thrombotic occlusion

explanation.summary: Statins reduce LDL cholesterol, stabilize coronary plaques, and provide primary prevention for myocardial infarction, reducing deaths from cardiac disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Statins (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors) offer primary prevention for myocardial infarction and reduce deaths from cardiac disease. They achieve this by reducing LDL cholesterol levels in the bloodstream and stabilizing plaques in coronary arteries. They should be prescribed to anyone with angina.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Statins do not directly relieve anginal pain or cause vasodilation; nitroglycerin and other vasodilators serve that function. Statins work by reducing cholesterol and stabilizing plaques over time.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Statins do not synergistically enhance beta-blocker effects on myocardial oxygen demand; they work through entirely different mechanisms (lipid reduction vs. adrenergic blockade).
whyWrongByChoice.D: Statins do not block calcium-dependent smooth muscle contraction; that mechanism describes calcium channel blockers, which are used for vasospastic angina.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Statins do not directly inhibit platelet aggregation; antiplatelet agents like aspirin and clopidogrel serve that function through cyclooxygenase inhibition and ADP receptor blockade.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with a history of unstable angina 2 months ago presents for follow-up. Her cardiologist notes that many experts now consider most cases of unstable angina to actually represent small myocardial infarctions. Which of the following findings best supports this evolving understanding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coronary angiography showing diffuse three-vessel atherosclerotic disease without acute plaque rupture

choices.label: B
choices.text: Absence of pathological Q waves on serial electrocardiograms obtained during the hospitalization period

choices.label: C
choices.text: Normal echocardiographic findings with preserved left ventricular ejection fraction after the acute event

choices.label: D
choices.text: Resolution of ST-segment depressions within 24 hours of initiating anticoagulation therapy with heparin

choices.label: E
choices.text: Elevated troponin levels detected by high-sensitivity assays in patients previously classified as having unstable angina

explanation.summary: High-sensitivity troponin assays reveal elevated cardiac enzymes in unstable angina, suggesting many cases actually represent small MIs, blurring the distinction between the two conditions.
explanation.whyCorrect: Many experts now believe that almost all cases of unstable angina represent small myocardial infarctions. This is because high-sensitivity troponin assays, which are more sensitive than traditional assays, usually show elevated troponin levels in unstable angina, just as they are in MIs. This has led to a decrease in the use of the term 'unstable angina' over time.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Diffuse atherosclerotic disease without plaque rupture would be more consistent with stable coronary artery disease rather than supporting the concept of unstable angina representing small MIs.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Preserved ejection fraction does not support the concept that unstable angina represents small MIs; in fact, small MIs may not significantly affect overall ejection fraction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Resolution of ST changes with treatment reflects response to therapy but does not address the underlying question of whether myocardial necrosis occurred.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Absence of Q waves indicates non-transmural injury, which is expected in small MIs; this finding alone does not distinguish unstable angina from small MIs.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with stable angina is taking sublingual nitroglycerin for acute symptom relief. His physician explains the mechanism by which nitroglycerin reduces chest pain. Which of the following best describes the primary hemodynamic effect of nitroglycerin that accounts for its antianginal action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased myocardial contractility through enhanced calcium influx into cardiac myocytes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct coronary artery dilation that increases oxygen delivery to ischemic myocardium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased preload through venodilation that reduces venous return and left ventricular filling

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inhibition of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system reducing systemic vascular resistance

choices.label: E
choices.text: Blockade of sympathetic nervous system activation reducing heart rate and blood pressure

explanation.summary: Nitroglycerin primarily targets veins, causing venodilation that decreases venous return and left ventricular filling (preload), thereby reducing myocardial oxygen demand.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nitroglycerin produces cGMP that relaxes vascular smooth muscle. It targets veins more than arteries. Dilating the large veins decreases venous pressure and venous return, which means decreased left ventricular filling (decreased preload). Decreased preload means decreased contraction force and myocardial oxygen use, thereby reducing chest pain. NTG also causes some direct coronary artery vasodilation, but preload reduction is the primary mechanism.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Nitroglycerin does not increase myocardial contractility or enhance calcium influx; positive inotropes like dobutamine have this effect. NTG reduces cardiac work through preload reduction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While nitroglycerin does cause some direct coronary artery dilation, this is a secondary mechanism; the primary antianginal effect is through venodilation and preload reduction that decreases myocardial oxygen demand.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nitroglycerin does not inhibit the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system; ACE inhibitors and ARBs work through that mechanism. NTG works via the nitric oxide-cGMP pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nitroglycerin does not block the sympathetic nervous system; beta-blockers have that effect. NTG works through direct vascular smooth muscle relaxation via cGMP.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with stable angina is prescribed ranolazine as an add-on therapy after inadequate symptom control with metoprolol and nitroglycerin. His physician warns him about a potential cardiac adverse effect of the medication and reviews his other prescriptions for drug interactions. Which of the following is the most important concern when prescribing ranolazine?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It can cause QT prolongation and has major interactions with CYP3A4 inhibitors that must be avoided

choices.label: B
choices.text: It may cause severe bradycardia when combined with beta-blocker therapy such as metoprolol

choices.label: C
choices.text: It frequently causes hepatotoxicity requiring regular monitoring of liver function enzymes

choices.label: D
choices.text: It produces reflex tachycardia that can worsen anginal symptoms during physical exertion

choices.label: E
choices.text: It causes severe postural hypotension similar to nitroglycerin requiring gradual dose titration

explanation.summary: Ranolazine can cause QT prolongation and is a CYP3A4 substrate, requiring avoidance of CYP3A4 inhibitors due to major drug interactions.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ranolazine inhibits late sodium current to reduce intracellular calcium overload and decrease myocardial oxygen demand. However, it can cause QT prolongation, a potentially dangerous cardiac adverse effect. Additionally, ranolazine is a CYP3A4 substrate, meaning CYP3A4 inhibitors must be avoided due to major drug interactions that could increase ranolazine levels and QT prolongation risk.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Ranolazine does not cause severe bradycardia; it works by inhibiting late sodium current, not by affecting heart rate through beta-adrenergic or calcium channel mechanisms.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hepatotoxicity with regular liver enzyme monitoring is not a major concern with ranolazine; QT prolongation and CYP3A4 interactions are the primary safety considerations.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ranolazine does not cause reflex tachycardia; it works through a unique mechanism (late sodium current inhibition) that does not produce the vasodilation-mediated reflex tachycardia seen with some other agents.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ranolazine does not cause severe postural hypotension; this adverse effect is characteristic of nitroglycerin due to its venodilatory properties.

questions.stem: A 54-year-old man with a history of stable angina presents to the emergency department with new-onset chest pain at rest that has been increasing in frequency over the past week. Previously, his angina occurred only with climbing two flights of stairs and resolved with rest. Today, his ECG shows ST-segment depressions and T-wave inversions in the anterolateral leads. Traditional troponin assays are negative. His cardiologist plans coronary angiography. Within what timeframe should angiography typically be performed for this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediately upon arrival, as would be done for an ST-elevation myocardial infarction

choices.label: B
choices.text: As an outpatient within 2 weeks of discharge after adequate medical management

choices.label: C
choices.text: Within 72 hours of admission after stabilization with medical therapy alone

choices.label: D
choices.text: Within 24 hours of arrival at the emergency room for evaluation and potential intervention

choices.label: E
choices.text: Only if the patient fails to respond to 6 weeks of maximal medical therapy

explanation.summary: Most patients with unstable angina will undergo coronary angiography within 24 hours of arrival at the emergency room, but it need not be done immediately as for ST-elevation MIs.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient's presentation—worsening angina now occurring at rest with ECG changes—is consistent with unstable angina. Most patients with unstable angina will undergo angiography within 24 hours of arrival at the emergency room. Unlike ST-elevation myocardial infarction, immediate (emergent) angiography is not required.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Immediate angiography upon arrival is the standard for ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), not for unstable angina; this patient's presentation with ST depressions and negative troponins does not require this level of urgency.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Waiting 72 hours would be unnecessarily delayed for unstable angina; the standard is to perform angiography within 24 hours to assess and potentially treat the coronary lesion.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Outpatient angiography within 2 weeks would be appropriate for stable angina workup, not for unstable angina, which requires urgent in-patient evaluation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Waiting 6 weeks for medical therapy to fail is appropriate for stable angina management but is dangerously delayed for unstable angina, which carries high mortality risk and requires urgent intervention.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension presents to the emergency department with 45 minutes of substernal chest pressure radiating to his left arm. An ECG shows ST-segment elevations in leads II, III, and aVF. He undergoes emergent percutaneous coronary intervention with successful stent placement. Twelve hours later, a repeat troponin level is markedly elevated. A myocardial biopsy from the infarcted region would most likely show which of the following histologic findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thick, intensely eosinophilic bands spanning the short axis of myocytes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Granulation tissue with fibroblast proliferation and neovascularization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Coagulative necrosis with a dense neutrophilic inflammatory infiltrate

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mature collagenous scar with complete replacement of myocardium

choices.label: E
choices.text: Caseating granulomas with multinucleated giant cells and central necrosis

explanation.summary: After successful reperfusion therapy, the influx of calcium from restored blood flow causes uncontrolled contraction of muscle fibers, producing contraction band necrosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Contraction band necrosis is the hallmark histologic finding of reperfusion injury. It appears as thick, intensely eosinophilic staining bands spanning the short axis of myocytes. This occurs because the influx of calcium from the reperfused vessel causes uncontrolled contraction of the muscle fibers.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Granulation tissue with fibroblasts and new blood vessels characterizes the 1- to 8-week period after MI, not the acute period following reperfusion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Coagulative necrosis with neutrophilic infiltrate is seen 1-3 days after MI without reperfusion; reperfusion injury specifically produces contraction band necrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mature collagenous scar formation occurs more than 8 weeks after MI, far later than 12 hours post-infarction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Caseating granulomas are characteristic of tuberculosis, not myocardial infarction or reperfusion injury.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with a history of well-controlled hypertension and diabetes mellitus presents to her primary care physician with complaints of intermittent nausea, epigastric discomfort, and fatigue over the past 2 days. She denies chest pain. Physical examination reveals mild diaphoresis. An ECG shows new T-wave inversions in leads V1-V4. Serum troponin I is elevated. Which of the following patient characteristics most likely explains why this patient presented without chest pain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Her history of well-controlled hypertension with normal blood pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Her sex and history of diabetes mellitus altering pain perception

choices.label: C
choices.text: The anatomic location of her myocardial infarction on the anterior wall

choices.label: D
choices.text: The absence of ST-segment elevation indicating a smaller infarct size

choices.label: E
choices.text: Her age indicating she has developed collateral coronary circulation

explanation.summary: Women, older patients, and those with diabetes mellitus are more likely to present with atypical MI symptoms such as nausea, epigastric pain, and dyspnea rather than classic chest pain.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material specifically identifies women, older patients, and those with diabetes mellitus as populations that may not experience chest pain during MI. Instead, they present with less-specific symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, epigastric pain, and weakness.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Well-controlled hypertension does not explain the absence of chest pain during MI. Hypertension is a risk factor for MI but does not influence pain perception.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The anatomic location of the MI does not determine whether the patient experiences chest pain or presents atypically.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While NSTEMIs are generally nontransmural, the subendocardial location does not explain atypical presentation; both STEMI and NSTEMI can present with or without chest pain.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Development of collateral circulation is not the reason this demographic presents atypically; diabetic autonomic neuropathy and sex-related differences in pain perception are more likely explanations.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man is hospitalized after an anterior wall ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction treated with percutaneous coronary intervention. On postoperative day 5, he develops sudden hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and jugular venous distension. Emergent echocardiography reveals fluid in the pericardial space. Which of the following best explains the timing of this complication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mature scar tissue has formed and is compressing adjacent structures

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ongoing coagulative necrosis is generating inflammatory exudate

choices.label: C
choices.text: Macrophages have removed dead myocytes but scar has not yet formed

choices.label: D
choices.text: Granulation tissue has undergone excessive fibrosis causing rigidity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Neutrophilic infiltration has caused abscess formation in the myocardium

explanation.summary: Between days 3-10 post-MI, macrophages remove dead myocytes but scar tissue has not yet replaced them, making the myocardium weakest and most susceptible to rupture.
explanation.whyCorrect: At 3-10 days post-MI, macrophages appear and remove dead heart cells, but scar tissue has not yet formed to replace them. This makes the tissue at its highest risk for rupture during this window. Ventricular wall rupture causes hemopericardium, presenting as cardiac tamponade.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mature scar formation occurs after 8 weeks, not at day 5. Scar tissue strengthens the area rather than causing rupture.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While coagulative necrosis is present in the first 1-3 days, the key factor at day 5 is the removal of dead tissue by macrophages without scar replacement.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Granulation tissue with fibroblasts appears at 1-8 weeks post-MI, and excessive fibrosis does not cause wall rupture.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Neutrophilic infiltration is maximal at 1-3 days post-MI and does not cause abscess formation; neutrophils clean up debris from dead myocytes.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after collapsing at work. His coworkers report that he complained of severe chest tightness before losing consciousness. He has no prior medical history but smokes 1 pack of cigarettes per day and has a family history of a father who died of a heart attack at age 50. An ECG shows ventricular fibrillation. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation is initiated. Which of the following is the most common cause of death within the first 72 hours after acute myocardial infarction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Papillary muscle rupture leading to acute mitral regurgitation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mural thrombus formation with embolization to the cerebral vasculature

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ventricular free wall rupture with resultant hemopericardium

choices.label: D
choices.text: Arrhythmias due to damage to the electrical conduction pathway

choices.label: E
choices.text: Systolic heart failure from decreased myocardial contractility

explanation.summary: Arrhythmias are the most common cause of death within the first 72 hours after acute MI, resulting from damage to the electrical conduction pathway.
explanation.whyCorrect: Most patients with an MI experience ventricular arrhythmias such as premature ventricular beats, ventricular fibrillation, or ventricular tachycardia. Arrhythmias are explicitly identified as the most common cause of death within the first 72 hours after acute MI.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Papillary muscle rupture typically occurs in the 2-7 day window post-MI and is not the most common cause of early death.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Mural thrombus formation is a thrombotic complication most common after anterior wall or apical infarcts but is not the most common cause of death in the first 72 hours.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ventricular free wall rupture typically occurs at 3-10 days post-MI when macrophages have weakened the tissue.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Heart failure from decreased contractility is most common with large infarcts and is a leading cause of long-term morbidity, but not the most common cause of death within the first 72 hours.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute-onset substernal chest pain after using cocaine at a party 2 hours ago. His ECG shows ST-segment elevations in leads V1-V4. He has no history of hypertension, diabetes, or hyperlipidemia. Coronary angiography reveals a thrombus in the left anterior descending artery with minimal underlying atherosclerosis. Which of the following mechanisms best explains how cocaine contributed to this patient's myocardial infarction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inhibition of cyclooxygenase leading to decreased prostacyclin production

choices.label: B
choices.text: Direct toxic injury to cardiac myocytes causing myocardial cell death

choices.label: C
choices.text: Stimulation of plasminogen activator inhibitor-1 preventing thrombolysis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acceleration of atherosclerotic plaque formation through lipid accumulation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased myocardial oxygen demand, coronary vasospasm, and platelet activation

explanation.summary: Cocaine causes MI through multiple mechanisms: increasing myocardial oxygen demand, causing coronary vasospasm, disrupting the endothelium, and activating platelets leading to thrombosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that cocaine use can increase myocardial oxygen demand, cause coronary vasospasm, and lead to acute MI. Cocaine also disrupts the endothelium and activates platelets, which can lead to plaque-rupture and thrombosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Inhibition of cyclooxygenase is the mechanism of aspirin, not cocaine. Cocaine's effects on platelets involve direct platelet activation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Direct toxic injury to cardiac myocytes is not the described mechanism by which cocaine causes MI; rather, it causes ischemia through vascular and platelet effects.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While PAI-1 is involved in thrombolysis regulation, cocaine's primary pathogenic mechanism involves vasospasm, increased oxygen demand, and platelet activation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cocaine causes acute MI through vasospasm and thrombosis, not through chronic acceleration of atherosclerotic plaque formation.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents to the emergency department with 3 hours of crushing substernal chest pain. An ECG shows ST-segment depressions and T-wave inversions in leads V3-V6. Serial troponin levels are elevated. A diagnosis of NSTEMI is made. Which of the following best describes the typical extent of myocardial damage in this patient's type of infarction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nontransmural necrosis typically involving the subendocardial myocardium

choices.label: B
choices.text: Transmural necrosis extending from endocardium to epicardium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Isolated epicardial necrosis with sparing of the subendocardial region

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patchy necrosis involving all layers of the myocardium equally

choices.label: E
choices.text: Selective necrosis of the conduction system with preserved contractile myocardium

explanation.summary: NSTEMI causes nontransmural infarction, typically involving only the subendocardial myocardium, which is farthest from the blood supply and most vulnerable to ischemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: NSTEMIs are generally nontransmural MIs, typically involving only the subendocardial myocardium. The subendocardial muscle is farthest from the blood supply, making this region the most vulnerable to ischemia and infarction.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Transmural necrosis extending from endocardium to epicardium is characteristic of STEMI, not NSTEMI.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Isolated epicardial necrosis is not a recognized pattern of MI. The subendocardium is more vulnerable because it is farthest from the blood supply.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Patchy necrosis involving all layers equally is not the characteristic pattern of NSTEMI.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Selective necrosis of the conduction system alone is not the typical pattern of NSTEMI.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with 1 hour of severe substernal chest pain. He has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. His initial troponin I level drawn on arrival is within normal limits. An ECG shows ST-segment elevations in leads V1-V4. The emergency physician initiates treatment for STEMI despite the negative troponin result. Which of the following best explains why the initial troponin level may be negative in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The patient has an NSTEMI rather than a STEMI based on the normal troponin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Troponin is only released when the entire myocardial wall thickness is necrotic

choices.label: C
choices.text: Troponin levels are only elevated in patients with underlying coronary artery disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: The ST-segment elevations indicate pericarditis rather than myocardial infarction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Insufficient time has elapsed for enough myocyte death to raise serum troponin levels
