explanation.summary: Cardiac biomarkers such as troponin become detectable in blood 4-6 hours after MI onset. With only 1 hour of symptoms, insufficient myocyte death has occurred to elevate serum troponin.
explanation.whyCorrect: After 4 to 6 hours, troponin and CK-MB become detectable in the blood. One negative troponin test on ER arrival does not rule out MI, and serial testing is needed. The clinical history and ECG findings are sufficient to diagnose STEMI and initiate emergent therapy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The ECG shows classic ST-segment elevations, which diagnose STEMI. A negative initial troponin does not change this diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Troponin is released whenever cardiac myocytes die, regardless of whether the necrosis is transmural or subendocardial.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Troponin is released from dying cardiac myocytes regardless of the underlying etiology and can be elevated in myocarditis and cardiac trauma.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Focal ST-segment elevations in V1-V4 with acute chest pain are consistent with anterior STEMI. Pericarditis typically causes diffuse ST elevations.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man is admitted to the coronary care unit after an anterior wall STEMI treated with percutaneous coronary intervention. On day 4 of hospitalization, a new holosystolic murmur is auscultated at the cardiac apex, and the patient develops acute pulmonary edema. Which of the following is the most likely cause of these findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Development of Dressler syndrome with pericardial effusion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Formation of a mural thrombus in the left ventricular apex

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acute mitral regurgitation from papillary muscle rupture

choices.label: D
choices.text: Progressive systolic heart failure from loss of contractile myocardium

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ventricular septal rupture with acute left-to-right shunting of blood

explanation.summary: A new holosystolic murmur with acute pulmonary edema 4 days post-MI is characteristic of papillary muscle rupture causing acute mitral regurgitation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Papillary muscle rupture is a mechanical complication of MI that occurs in the 2-7 day window. Symptoms of acute mitral regurgitation include a new holosystolic murmur and pulmonary edema.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Dressler syndrome causes pericarditis with fever, pleurisy, and pleural effusion but does not cause a holosystolic murmur or acute pulmonary edema.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Mural thrombus formation can lead to embolization but does not cause a new holosystolic murmur or acute pulmonary edema.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While progressive heart failure can cause pulmonary edema, the acute onset of a new holosystolic murmur points to a mechanical complication.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ventricular septal rupture produces a harsh systolic murmur best heard at the left sternal border, not at the apex.

questions.stem: A pathologist examines a myocardial biopsy specimen from a patient who died 6 weeks after an acute myocardial infarction. Which of the following histologic findings is most expected in the infarcted region?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hyper-eosinophilic cells maintaining cellular outlines with loss of nuclei

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dense neutrophilic infiltrate surrounding areas of coagulative necrosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Macrophages actively phagocytizing necrotic myocyte debris

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fibroblasts with collagen deposition and new blood vessel formation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Multiple layers of mature collagen forming a dense fibrous scar

explanation.summary: At 1-8 weeks post-MI, granulation tissue forms with fibroblasts laying down collagen and new blood vessels growing.
explanation.whyCorrect: During the 1-8 week window, fibroblasts lay down collagen and create a framework for scar formation, aided by growth of new blood vessels. The combination of fibroblasts, new blood vessels, and collagen deposition is called granulation tissue. At 6 weeks, this phase is still active.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hyper-eosinophilic cells with maintained outlines and lost nuclei describe coagulative necrosis seen in the first 0-24 hours after MI.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Dense neutrophilic infiltrate with coagulative necrosis is characteristic of the 1-3 day time period after MI.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Macrophages actively phagocytizing debris is the hallmark of the 3-10 day period post-MI.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mature collagenous scar forms after more than 8 weeks post-MI. At 6 weeks, scar formation is still ongoing.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with high fever, bilateral nonexudative conjunctivitis, a polymorphous rash, and swollen hands and feet. He is diagnosed with a medium-vessel vasculitis. His parents are counseled that their son is at risk for a potentially life-threatening cardiac complication. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which this child's condition can lead to myocardial infarction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coronary artery vasospasm triggered by inflammatory mediator release

choices.label: B
choices.text: Coronary artery aneurysm formation with subsequent thrombosis and occlusion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Direct invasion of myocardial cells by inflammatory cells causing necrosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Accelerated coronary atherosclerosis from chronic endothelial inflammation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aortic root dilation leading to compression of the coronary artery ostia

explanation.summary: Kawasaki disease causes coronary artery aneurysms that can lead to thrombosis and artery occlusion, resulting in downstream myocardial infarction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Kawasaki disease is characterized by coronary artery aneurysms. Resultant thrombosis and artery occlusion can cause downstream myocardial infarction. Children with Kawasaki disease should take aspirin to prevent MI.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Coronary vasospasm is a recognized cause of MI but is not the mechanism associated with Kawasaki disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Direct myocardial invasion describes myocarditis, not the mechanism of MI in Kawasaki disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Kawasaki disease causes aneurysms rather than accelerated atherosclerotic plaque formation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aortic root dilation with coronary ostial compression is not a feature of Kawasaki disease.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with a history of anterior wall ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction 2 weeks ago presents with sudden onset of right-sided weakness and slurred speech. CT of the head shows an acute ischemic stroke in the left middle cerebral artery territory. Echocardiography reveals a mass attached to the endocardial surface of the left ventricular apex. Which of the following best explains the pathogenesis of this patient's stroke?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Atrial fibrillation secondary to post-MI conduction system damage

choices.label: B
choices.text: Paradoxical embolism through a patent foramen ovale from a deep vein thrombus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Carotid artery atherosclerotic plaque rupture with in situ thrombosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Embolization of a mural thrombus from the left ventricular wall

choices.label: E
choices.text: Infective endocarditis with septic emboli from the mitral valve

explanation.summary: Mural thrombus formation on the endocardial surface is most common after anterior wall or apical left ventricular infarcts. Embolization to the brain causes ischemic stroke.
explanation.whyCorrect: Patients after MI may develop a mural thrombus attached to the endocardium, most commonly after infarcts in the left ventricular anterior wall or apex. These patients require anticoagulation to prevent embolization to the brain or extremities.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While atrial fibrillation can cause stroke, the echocardiographic finding of a mass at the left ventricular apex is diagnostic of mural thrombus.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Paradoxical embolism would not explain the echocardiographic finding of a left ventricular apical mass.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Carotid atherosclerotic plaque rupture would not explain the left ventricular mass on echocardiography.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Infective endocarditis involves valve vegetations, not a mass at the ventricular apex. There are no signs of infection.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute substernal chest pressure. His ECG shows ST-segment elevations in leads V2 and V3. According to the diagnostic criteria for STEMI, what is the minimum ST-segment elevation threshold in leads V2-V3 that meets criteria for STEMI in a man of this age?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Greater than or equal to 1.0 mm in two contiguous leads

choices.label: B
choices.text: Greater than or equal to 1.5 mm regardless of patient age or sex

choices.label: C
choices.text: Greater than or equal to 2.0 mm in men 40 years or older

choices.label: D
choices.text: Greater than or equal to 3.0 mm in any lead for all patients

choices.label: E
choices.text: Greater than or equal to 2.5 mm in men younger than 40 years

explanation.summary: For anterior leads V2-V3, STEMI diagnostic criteria are age- and sex-specific: 2.5 mm or more in men younger than 40.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material specifies that for anterior leads V2-V3, the criteria for ST elevation is 2.5 mm or more in men younger than 40 years.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While 1 mm ST elevation in two contiguous leads is the general criterion, leads V2-V3 have specific age- and sex-based criteria requiring higher thresholds.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The 1.5 mm threshold applies specifically to women regardless of age, not to all patients.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The 2.0 mm threshold is correct for men aged 40 years or older, not for men younger than 40.
whyWrongByChoice.D: There is no 3.0 mm threshold in the diagnostic criteria for STEMI.

questions.stem: A 59-year-old man is found dead at home 3 days after experiencing severe chest pain that he did not seek medical attention for. At autopsy, the coronary arteries show a ruptured atherosclerotic plaque with an overlying thrombus in the left anterior descending artery. Histologic examination of the plaque reveals a thin fibrous cap, a large lipid core, and numerous inflammatory cells. Which of the following features of the plaque most directly contributed to its instability and rupture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Presence of inflammatory cells and matrix-degrading enzymes weakening the cap

choices.label: B
choices.text: Calcification of the plaque causing rigidity and subsequent fracture

choices.label: C
choices.text: Excessive smooth muscle cell proliferation expanding the plaque volume

choices.label: D
choices.text: External compression of the artery by surrounding mediastinal structures

choices.label: E
choices.text: Neovascularization within the plaque leading to intraplaque hemorrhage

explanation.summary: Vulnerable atherosclerotic plaques have thin fibrous caps, large lipid cores, and inflammatory cells with matrix-degrading enzymes that make them unstable and prone to rupture.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that the presence of inflammatory cells and matrix-degrading enzymes make vulnerable plaques unstable, increasing the risk of thin fibrous cap rupture.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Calcification is a feature of stable plaques and does not directly contribute to the type of plaque instability described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Smooth muscle cell proliferation contributes to plaque growth but the key instability factors are the thin cap, large lipid core, and inflammatory cells.
whyWrongByChoice.D: External compression is not a recognized mechanism of atherosclerotic plaque rupture.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While neovascularization can contribute, the source specifically identifies inflammatory cells and matrix-degrading enzymes as the key instability factors.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man had a myocardial infarction 10 weeks ago that was treated with percutaneous coronary intervention. He now presents for follow-up. An ECG shows persistent abnormal Q waves in the anterior leads. Which of the following properties of the mature post-MI scar best explains why his ECG has not returned to normal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The scar actively generates abnormal electrical impulses that cause Q waves

choices.label: B
choices.text: The scar is not contractile and does not conduct electricity like normal myocytes

choices.label: C
choices.text: The scar tissue compresses adjacent coronary arteries causing ongoing ischemia

choices.label: D
choices.text: The scar releases persistent inflammatory mediators altering depolarization

choices.label: E
choices.text: The scar causes chronic pericardial irritation mimicking ST-segment changes

explanation.summary: Post-MI scar tissue is composed of collagen that is neither contractile nor electrically conductive, leading to chronic ECG abnormalities such as abnormal Q waves.
explanation.whyCorrect: The scar is composed of multiple layers of collagen. It is not contractile like the myocardium and does not conduct electricity like normal myocytes do. This leads to chronic ECG abnormalities such as abnormal Q waves.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Scar tissue does not actively generate electrical impulses. It is electrically inert, which is why abnormal Q waves develop.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Scar tissue does not compress adjacent coronary arteries. The Q waves result from the inability of scar tissue to conduct electrical activity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mature scar tissue at 10 weeks does not release inflammatory mediators.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chronic pericardial irritation is not caused by mature scar tissue and would produce ST-segment changes, not persistent Q waves.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man is admitted with an acute ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction. Three days later, he develops a low-grade fever, pleuritic chest pain that improves when leaning forward, and a friction rub on cardiac auscultation. Which of the following best explains the timing and mechanism of this complication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autoimmune pericarditis from Dressler syndrome occurring weeks after MI

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bacterial endocarditis from catheter-related infection during intervention

choices.label: C
choices.text: Transmural ischemia extending to the epicardium causing acute pericarditis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pulmonary embolism from deep venous thrombosis during hospitalization

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ventricular wall rupture with blood accumulating in the pericardial sac

explanation.summary: Post-MI pericarditis occurs when transmural ischemia extends through the myocardial wall to the epicardium, typically 24-96 hours post-MI.
explanation.whyCorrect: Inflammation may cause pericarditis when the ischemia extends through the myocardial wall to the epicardium. This usually happens 24 to 96 hours post-MI.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Dressler syndrome occurs several days to weeks post-MI. At only 3 days post-MI, acute pericarditis from transmural inflammation is more likely.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Bacterial endocarditis would present with high fever and positive blood cultures, not pleuritic chest pain with a friction rub.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pulmonary embolism would present with dyspnea and tachycardia but would not cause a pericardial friction rub.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ventricular wall rupture would present with sudden hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and JVD, not gradual pleuritic chest pain.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man presents with acute chest pain. His initial troponin is negative, but CK-MB is mildly elevated. He had been treated for a confirmed STEMI 5 days ago. The emergency physician suspects a new cardiac event. Which of the following best explains why CK-MB is a more useful marker than troponin in this clinical scenario?

choices.label: A
choices.text: CK-MB is more specific for cardiac tissue than troponin I or troponin T

choices.label: B
choices.text: CK-MB rises earlier than troponin after the onset of myocardial necrosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: CK-MB is only elevated in myocardial infarction, whereas troponin rises in other conditions

choices.label: D
choices.text: CK-MB levels correlate more accurately with the size of the myocardial infarction

choices.label: E
choices.text: CK-MB normalizes sooner than troponin, making it useful for detecting reinfarction

explanation.summary: CK-MB normalizes sooner than troponins after MI, so a re-elevation can detect reinfarction while troponin may still be elevated from the initial event.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because CK-MB normalizes sooner than troponins, it may be a useful marker for detecting reinfarction. Troponin may still be elevated from the initial STEMI 5 days ago.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CK-MB is less specific for cardiac tissue than troponin. Cardiac troponin I and T are specific to cardiac myocytes.
whyWrongByChoice.B: CK-MB and troponin both become detectable at approximately 4-6 hours. CK-MB does not have a significantly earlier rise time.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CK-MB is not exclusive to myocardial infarction and can be elevated in skeletal muscle injury.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While CK-MB levels can correlate with infarct size, this is not the reason it is more useful for detecting reinfarction.

questions.stem: A 61-year-old man with a history of erectile dysfunction treated with sildenafil presents to the emergency department with crushing substernal chest pain. His ECG shows ST-segment elevations in the inferior leads. His blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg. The emergency physician considers administering sublingual nitroglycerin for pain relief. Which of the following is the most important reason to withhold nitroglycerin in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nitroglycerin is contraindicated in patients with inferior wall STEMI

choices.label: B
choices.text: Drug interaction between nitroglycerin and phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nitroglycerin has no proven mortality benefit in acute coronary syndrome

choices.label: D
choices.text: The patient's blood pressure is too high for safe nitroglycerin administration

choices.label: E
choices.text: Nitroglycerin should only be given intravenously, not sublingually, in STEMI

explanation.summary: Nitroglycerin has a critical drug interaction with phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors such as sildenafil, which can cause severe hypotension.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material specifically notes that an important drug interaction exists between nitrates and phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors such as sildenafil (Viagra). Both drugs cause vasodilation, and their combination can lead to dangerous hypotension.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Nitroglycerin is not absolutely contraindicated in inferior wall STEMI. It is used in hemodynamically stable patients with ACS.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While nitroglycerin does not have proven mortality benefit, the dangerous drug interaction with sildenafil is the primary concern here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A blood pressure of 135/85 mmHg would be acceptable for nitroglycerin if not for the sildenafil interaction. Nitrates are used when SBP is 90 mmHg or greater.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nitroglycerin is appropriately administered sublingually in ACS for immediate pain relief.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man presents with an acute inferior wall STEMI. The nearest percutaneous coronary intervention-capable facility is 3 hours away by ambulance transport. The local emergency department physician decides to administer a thrombolytic agent instead. Which of the following best describes the ideal timing for administering thrombolytic therapy in this scenario?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Within 30 minutes of first contact with a medical team

choices.label: B
choices.text: Within 90 minutes of initial symptom onset by the patient

choices.label: C
choices.text: Within 6 hours of symptom onset for maximum efficacy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Within 24 hours of symptom onset if troponin remains elevated

choices.label: E
choices.text: Within 48 hours of symptom onset alongside anticoagulant therapy

explanation.summary: Thrombolytic agents should ideally be given within 30 minutes of first contact with a medical team, and must be given within 12 hours of symptom onset.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that thrombolytic drugs should be given within 12 hours of symptom onset, ideally within 30 minutes of first contact with a medical team, because effectiveness decreases with time.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The 90-minute window refers to the ideal door-to-balloon time for PCI, not thrombolytic therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While thrombolytics can be effective up to 12 hours after symptom onset, the ideal timing is within 30 minutes of first medical contact.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Administration beyond 12 hours has no demonstrated benefit. There is no 24-hour window for thrombolytic therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: There is no 48-hour window for thrombolytic therapy. Effectiveness decreases with time.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old woman is diagnosed with NSTEMI in the emergency department. She is started on appropriate antiplatelet and anticoagulant therapy. The cardiology team plans for coronary angiography. Which of the following best describes why thrombolytic therapy is NOT indicated for this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Thrombolytics are only effective for clots older than 12 hours in duration

choices.label: B
choices.text: Thrombolytics are contraindicated in women due to increased bleeding risk

choices.label: C
choices.text: NSTEMI patients have unstable angina and do not have actual infarction

choices.label: D
choices.text: The coronary occlusion is not complete and studies show no improved outcome

choices.label: E
choices.text: Thrombolytics require concurrent PCI which is planned for a later time

explanation.summary: Thrombolytics are not used for NSTEMI because the coronary occlusion is incomplete and studies show no overall improved outcome.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that thrombolytics are not used for NSTEMI because the occlusion is not complete and studies show no overall improved outcome, therefore the risks exceed benefits.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Thrombolytics are most effective when given early, not for old clots. They should be used within 12 hours of symptom onset.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Thrombolytics are not contraindicated based on sex.
whyWrongByChoice.C: NSTEMI is distinct from unstable angina. NSTEMI does involve actual myocardial infarction with elevated troponins.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thrombolytics do not require concurrent PCI. They are an alternative to PCI for STEMI when PCI is unavailable.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with a history of asthma is admitted to the coronary care unit with an acute STEMI. After successful PCI, the physician considers initiating beta-blocker therapy. The patient's heart rate is 78 bpm and blood pressure is 128/76 mmHg. Which of the following beta-blockers would be most appropriate for this patient given his comorbidity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Propranolol, a nonselective beta-blocker with proven post-MI benefit

choices.label: B
choices.text: Labetalol, a combined alpha- and beta-blocker for blood pressure control

choices.label: C
choices.text: Metoprolol, a cardioselective beta-1 blocker minimizing bronchospasm risk

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nadolol, a long-acting nonselective beta-blocker for sustained heart rate control

choices.label: E
choices.text: Timolol, a nonselective beta-blocker commonly used after myocardial infarction

explanation.summary: Cardioselective beta-1 blockers such as metoprolol are preferred in patients with asthma because they minimize the risk of bronchospasm from beta-2 blockade.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that cardioselective beta-1 blockers are preferred because selective agents prevent side effects such as bronchospasm that can occur with beta-2 inhibition in patients with asthma. Metoprolol is specifically listed as one of the agents used in ACS.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Propranolol is a nonselective beta-blocker that can cause bronchospasm in patients with asthma.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Labetalol blocks both beta-1 and beta-2 receptors and could trigger bronchospasm.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Nadolol is a nonselective beta-blocker and could precipitate bronchospasm in asthmatic patients.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Timolol is a nonselective beta-blocker and carries the risk of bronchoconstriction in asthmatic patients.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man is admitted with an NSTEMI and started on intravenous unfractionated heparin along with aspirin and clopidogrel. On hospital day 5, his platelet count drops from 240,000/microL to 85,000/microL. He develops new swelling and pain in his left calf. Which of the following best describes the mechanism underlying his thrombocytopenia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct bone marrow suppression from heparin reducing platelet production

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aspirin-induced irreversible platelet inhibition causing accelerated clearance

choices.label: C
choices.text: Disseminated intravascular coagulation from ongoing myocardial necrosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Immune-mediated platelet destruction with paradoxical hypercoagulability

choices.label: E
choices.text: Clopidogrel-induced thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura with platelet consumption

explanation.summary: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) is an immune response causing platelet destruction and paradoxical hypercoagulability.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material describes HIT as an immune response causing platelet destruction and hypercoagulability. HIT occurs more commonly with intravenous heparin and is an absolute contraindication to further heparin use. The dropping platelet count with new DVT is classic for HIT.

whyWrongByChoice.A: HIT is not caused by bone marrow suppression. It is an immune-mediated process.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While aspirin irreversibly inhibits platelet function, it does not cause significant thrombocytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DIC would present with both thrombocytopenia and bleeding. The isolated platelet drop with new thrombosis is more consistent with HIT.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While clopidogrel can rarely cause TTP, the temporal association with heparin and the pattern of thrombocytopenia with thrombosis is most consistent with HIT.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man with a history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia presents with an acute STEMI requiring emergent percutaneous coronary intervention. Heparin is absolutely contraindicated. Which of the following alternative anticoagulants is most appropriate for this patient during the procedure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enoxaparin, a low-molecular-weight heparin with predictable pharmacokinetics

choices.label: B
choices.text: Warfarin, an oral vitamin K antagonist with rapid onset of anticoagulation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Aspirin, an irreversible cyclooxygenase inhibitor providing anticoagulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ticagrelor, a reversible P2Y12 receptor antagonist with anticoagulant properties

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bivalirudin, a reversible direct thrombin inhibitor used during PCI

explanation.summary: Bivalirudin is a reversible direct thrombin inhibitor indicated when heparin is contraindicated in patients undergoing PCI.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that bivalirudin is a reversible direct thrombin inhibitor mostly used when heparin is contraindicated in patients undergoing PCI. It is an alternative for patients with HIT.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Enoxaparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin and is contraindicated in patients with HIT.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Warfarin has a slow onset of action (days) and is not appropriate for acute anticoagulation during emergent PCI.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aspirin is an antiplatelet agent, not an anticoagulant, and cannot replace heparin during PCI.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ticagrelor is a P2Y12 receptor antagonist that inhibits platelet activation but does not have anticoagulant properties.

questions.stem: A 59-year-old man presents with acute chest pain and is diagnosed with STEMI. He is immediately given aspirin 325 mg and instructed to chew the tablet rather than swallow it whole. Postprocedure, he is started on a P2Y12 inhibitor. At 15 months post-MI, he experiences dyspnea with his P2Y12 inhibitor. Which of the following P2Y12 inhibitors is most likely responsible for this side effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Clopidogrel, which commonly causes dyspnea in approximately 15% of patients

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prasugrel, which has a well-known side effect of significant shortness of breath

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ticagrelor, which causes significant shortness of breath in about 15% of patients

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cangrelor, which is an oral agent causing respiratory depression over time

choices.label: E
choices.text: Abciximab, which is a P2Y12 inhibitor associated with bronchospasm

explanation.summary: Ticagrelor has a unique adverse effect of causing significant shortness of breath in approximately 15% of patients.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material specifically states that ticagrelor has an additional concerning side effect: in about 15% of patients, it causes significant shortness of breath.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Clopidogrel's main adverse effect is bleeding, not dyspnea. The 15% dyspnea rate is specifically associated with ticagrelor.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Prasugrel's primary adverse effect is bleeding, not shortness of breath.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cangrelor is an intravenous (not oral) P2Y12 inhibitor and is not associated with the 15% dyspnea rate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Abciximab is a GP IIb/IIIa inhibitor, not a P2Y12 inhibitor.

questions.stem: A 73-year-old man presents with an acute STEMI. The interventional cardiologist notes that angiography reveals a significant stenosis of the left main coronary artery without evidence of collateral circulation. Which of the following is the most appropriate intervention for this lesion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Percutaneous coronary intervention with drug-eluting stent placement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intravenous thrombolytic therapy with tissue plasminogen activator

choices.label: C
choices.text: Medical management alone with antiplatelet and anticoagulant therapy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surgical coronary artery bypass for left main coronary artery disease

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intra-aortic balloon pump placement for hemodynamic stabilization

explanation.summary: When the left main coronary artery lacks collateral flow, PCI is contraindicated. These patients undergo surgical coronary artery bypass.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that when the LMCA lacks collateral flow, PCI should not be used because if the LMCA thromboses during the procedure, a large part of the myocardium can infarct. Patients with LMCA lesions instead undergo surgical coronary artery bypass.

whyWrongByChoice.A: PCI is contraindicated for LMCA lesions without collateral flow due to the risk of massive infarction if the artery thromboses during the procedure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Thrombolytic therapy does not address the underlying stenosis and is a backup to PCI, not a substitute for definitive revascularization.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Medical management alone is insufficient for significant left main coronary artery disease requiring definitive revascularization.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Intra-aortic balloon pump provides temporary hemodynamic support but does not address the underlying LMCA stenosis.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with crushing chest pain. His oxygen saturation is 97% on room air. The attending physician orders supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula. A medical student questions whether oxygen therapy is necessary. Which of the following best supports the student's concern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Oxygen therapy increases myocardial oxygen demand through coronary vasodilation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Research shows no benefit and possible harm from oxygen in nonhypoxic MI patients

choices.label: C
choices.text: Supplemental oxygen interferes with the absorption of antiplatelet medications

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oxygen therapy masks chest pain symptoms needed for monitoring ischemia progression

choices.label: E
choices.text: Supplemental oxygen is only indicated for patients with confirmed STEMI on ECG

explanation.summary: Research shows that giving oxygen to nonhypoxic MI patients has no benefit and may cause harm. Oxygen should only be used when saturation is less than 90%.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that most MI patients have no hypoxemia and do not benefit from oxygen therapy. Research shows no benefit and may cause harm in nonhypoxic patients. It should only be used when oxygen saturation is less than 90%.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The concern with unnecessary oxygen is lack of benefit and potential harm, not increased myocardial oxygen demand.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Interference with antiplatelet medication absorption is attributed to morphine, not oxygen therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Masking pain symptoms is a concern with morphine use, not oxygen therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Oxygen indication is based on hypoxemia (saturation less than 90%), not the type of ACS.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old man is diagnosed with STEMI and undergoes emergent PCI with placement of a drug-eluting stent in the right coronary artery. Which of the following best describes the primary purpose of the drug coating on the stent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prevention of thrombus formation on the stent surface through anticoagulation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Dissolution of residual thrombus within the stented coronary artery segment

choices.label: C
choices.text: Delivery of antiplatelet medication directly to the site of plaque rupture

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduction of inflammatory mediators to prevent post-procedural pericarditis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inhibition of neointimal proliferation to reduce the risk of restenosis

explanation.summary: Drug-eluting stents have a drug bonded to a polymer coating that limits neointimal proliferation, reducing the risk of restenosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that drug-eluting stents have a drug bonded to a polymer coating that limits neointimal proliferation and hence the risk of restenosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The drug on drug-eluting stents is not an anticoagulant. Anticoagulation is achieved through systemic medications.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Drug-eluting stents do not dissolve thrombus. Thrombus removal is through the mechanical action of angioplasty.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The drug coating is not an antiplatelet agent delivered locally. Antiplatelet therapy is provided systemically.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The drug coating does not target post-procedural pericarditis. Its purpose is preventing restenosis.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man is admitted with an NSTEMI. He is started on aspirin, clopidogrel, heparin, metoprolol, and atorvastatin. He undergoes PCI with stent placement on hospital day 2. The resident asks why atorvastatin was started acutely rather than waiting until lipid panels returned. Which of the following best describes the immediate benefit of statin therapy in the acute period following MI?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anti-inflammatory and anti-thrombotic effects independent of lipid lowering

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rapid reduction of LDL cholesterol levels preventing further plaque formation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Direct coronary artery vasodilation improving myocardial blood flow

choices.label: D
choices.text: Enhancement of antiplatelet drug efficacy through pharmacokinetic interactions

choices.label: E
choices.text: Prevention of contrast-induced nephropathy during cardiac catheterization

explanation.summary: In the acute period following MI, statins provide immediate anti-inflammatory and anti-thrombotic effects independent of lipid lowering.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that in the acute period, statin's immediate effects are anti-inflammatory and anti-thrombotic, not lipid lowering.

whyWrongByChoice.B: While statins do reduce LDL cholesterol, this effect takes weeks. The immediate benefit is anti-inflammatory and anti-thrombotic.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Statins do not directly cause coronary vasodilation. That is achieved through nitrates.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Statins do not enhance antiplatelet drug efficacy through pharmacokinetic interactions.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Prevention of contrast-induced nephropathy is not described as a primary benefit of acute statin therapy in the source material.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with a suspected acute MI. In the emergency department, he is given aspirin, which he is instructed to chew rather than swallow whole. The attending physician emphasizes to the team that aspirin is the single most important initial medication. Which of the following best explains why aspirin is considered the gold standard first-line treatment for suspected MI?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It provides analgesia through COX inhibition, reducing the chest pain of MI

choices.label: B
choices.text: It activates the intrinsic fibrinolytic pathway to dissolve the coronary thrombus

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is the only medication in the MONA protocol with proven mortality benefit for ACS

choices.label: D
choices.text: It inhibits the coagulation cascade through direct inhibition of factor Xa

choices.label: E
choices.text: It reduces myocardial oxygen demand by decreasing heart rate and contractility

explanation.summary: Of the MONA medications (morphine, oxygen, nitrates, aspirin), only aspirin has a proven mortality benefit for ACS.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material explicitly states that of the four MONA medications, only aspirin has a proven mortality benefit for ACS. Morphine, oxygen, and nitrates can all be harmful.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While aspirin does inhibit COX, its importance in MI is as an antiplatelet agent, not as an analgesic.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Aspirin does not activate the fibrinolytic pathway. That is the mechanism of tissue plasminogen activator.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aspirin does not inhibit factor Xa. That is the mechanism of heparin and fondaparinux.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reducing myocardial oxygen demand through decreased heart rate and contractility is the mechanism of beta-blockers, not aspirin.

questions.stem: A 71-year-old man with an acute STEMI is being prepared for emergent PCI. The interventional cardiologist requests administration of a glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitor as an adjunct to the procedure. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which this class of drugs prevents thrombus formation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Irreversible inhibition of cyclooxygenase preventing thromboxane A2 synthesis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inhibition of the final step in platelet aggregation by blocking fibrinogen binding

choices.label: C
choices.text: Activation of antithrombin to enhance inactivation of factors Xa and thrombin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Conversion of plasminogen to plasmin promoting dissolution of fibrin clots

choices.label: E
choices.text: Blockade of the ADP receptor preventing intracellular cAMP reduction

explanation.summary: GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors antagonize the GP IIb/IIIa receptor, inhibiting the final step in platelet aggregation by preventing fibrinogen binding.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors antagonize the GP IIb/IIIa receptor and inhibit the final step in platelet aggregation. This receptor binds fibrinogen, which leads to linked platelets that form a clot.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Irreversible inhibition of cyclooxygenase is the mechanism of aspirin, not GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Activation of antithrombin is the mechanism of heparin and enoxaparin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Conversion of plasminogen to plasmin is the mechanism of thrombolytic agents such as tPA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Blockade of the ADP receptor is the mechanism of P2Y12 inhibitors such as clopidogrel and ticagrelor.

questions.stem: A 54-year-old man presents to the emergency department with an acute STEMI. He has a history of a hemorrhagic stroke 2 months ago. The emergency physician determines that both percutaneous coronary intervention and thrombolytic therapy carry significant risks. Which of the following is an absolute contraindication specifically to thrombolytic therapy that makes PCI the preferred approach despite its risks?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Recent intracranial hemorrhage within the preceding months

choices.label: B
choices.text: History of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia on prior admission

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic kidney disease with baseline creatinine of 2.5 mg/dL

choices.label: D
choices.text: Coronary artery stenosis greater than 50% on prior angiography

choices.label: E
choices.text: Active gastrointestinal bleeding from a known duodenal ulcer

explanation.summary: Intracranial hemorrhage is an absolute contraindication to thrombolytic therapy, making PCI the preferred intervention.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material lists intracranial hemorrhage as one of the absolute contraindications to thrombolytic therapy. Other absolute contraindications include active bleeding, aortic dissection, closed head trauma, recent ischemic stroke, and severe uncontrolled hypertension.

whyWrongByChoice.B: History of HIT is a contraindication to heparin, not specifically to thrombolytic therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chronic kidney disease is a consideration for contrast-induced nephropathy with PCI but is not listed as an absolute contraindication to thrombolytics.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Coronary artery stenosis greater than 50% is an indication for PCI, not a contraindication to thrombolytics.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While active bleeding is also an absolute contraindication to thrombolytics, the question specifically asks about the patient's history of intracranial hemorrhage, which is the most critical contraindication given the risk of recurrent brain hemorrhage.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a high-speed head-on motor vehicle collision. On arrival, he is tachycardic with a heart rate of 118 bpm and blood pressure of 94/62 mmHg. A supine anteroposterior chest radiograph reveals a mediastinal width of 10 cm and rightward tracheal deviation. The emergency physician orders an urgent imaging study. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnostic imaging?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chest CT angiogram timed for the arterial contrast phase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Transesophageal echocardiography at the bedside

choices.label: C
choices.text: Conventional coronary angiography via femoral access

choices.label: D
choices.text: Magnetic resonance angiography of the thoracic aorta

choices.label: E
choices.text: Posteroanterior and lateral chest radiograph series

explanation.summary: A widened mediastinum (>8 cm) with tracheal deviation after a rapid deceleration injury is highly concerning for traumatic aortic disruption, which requires emergent chest CT angiogram timed for the arterial phase.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chest CT angiogram is the appropriate emergent study to evaluate a widened mediastinum after blunt thoracic trauma. The arterial contrast phase optimally visualizes aortic injuries at the ligamentum arteriosum, where shear forces concentrate during sudden deceleration.

whyWrongByChoice.B: While echocardiography is useful for cardiac evaluation, it is not the standard emergent test for evaluating a widened mediastinum suspicious for traumatic aortic disruption.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Conventional coronary angiography evaluates coronary arteries for stenosis and is not indicated in the acute trauma setting for aortic injury evaluation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: MR angiography is time-consuming and not suitable for emergent evaluation of a potentially unstable trauma patient with suspected aortic disruption.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Additional plain radiographs would not provide sufficient detail to diagnose aortic disruption and would delay definitive imaging in this emergency.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with a history of chronic heart failure presents with worsening dyspnea over the past 3 days. Her chest radiograph shows cardiomegaly, prominent hilar vasculature, and short thin linear opacities approximately 1-2 cm long at the lung periphery extending to the pleural surface at right angles. These peripheral opacities most likely represent which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Alveolar consolidation from bacterial pneumonia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Thickening of the interlobular septa from interstitial edema

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bronchial wall thickening from chronic obstructive lung disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fibrotic scarring from prior granulomatous infection

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lymphangitic carcinomatosis from metastatic breast cancer

explanation.summary: The described findings are Kerley B lines, which represent thickening of interlobular septa containing lymphatic and venous vessels due to interstitial edema in the setting of cardiogenic pulmonary edema.
explanation.whyCorrect: Kerley B lines are short, thin, linear opacities (1-2 cm long) seen at the lung periphery extending to the pleural surface at right angles. They represent thickening of the interlobular septa, which contain lymphatic and venous vessels, due to interstitial edema from elevated pulmonary capillary pressures in heart failure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bacterial pneumonia causes alveolar consolidation that typically appears as focal or lobar opacification rather than short linear peripheral opacities at right angles to the pleural surface.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bronchial wall thickening from COPD presents as peribronchial cuffing or tramline shadows, not as short peripheral linear opacities perpendicular to the pleural surface.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fibrotic scarring from granulomatous disease typically appears as upper lobe reticular markings or calcified nodules, not as the described short peripheral linear opacities.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While lymphangitic carcinomatosis can cause interlobular septal thickening, the clinical context of chronic heart failure with cardiomegaly and hilar prominence makes cardiogenic interstitial edema far more likely.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man undergoes an echocardiogram that reveals a left ventricular end-diastolic volume of 160 mL and an end-systolic volume of 96 mL. Which of the following best represents this patient's ejection fraction?

explanation.summary: Ejection fraction is calculated as (End Diastolic Volume - End Systolic Volume) / End Diastolic Volume. Here: (160 - 96) / 160 = 64/160 = 0.40 or 40%, indicating reduced systolic function.
explanation.whyCorrect: EF = Stroke Volume / End Diastolic Volume = (EDV - ESV) / EDV = (160 - 96) / 160 = 64/160 = 40%. Normal EF is 55-70%, so this patient has a reduced ejection fraction consistent with systolic dysfunction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: An EF of 30% would require an ESV of 112 mL with an EDV of 160 mL, which does not match the given values.
whyWrongByChoice.C: An EF of 50% would require an ESV of 80 mL with an EDV of 160 mL, which does not match the given values.
whyWrongByChoice.D: An EF of 60% would require an ESV of 64 mL with an EDV of 160 mL, which does not match the given values.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An EF of 70% would require an ESV of 48 mL with an EDV of 160 mL, which does not match the given values.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman presents with progressive exertional dyspnea. Chest radiograph reveals an enlarged cardiac silhouette with a rounded, symmetrical shape described as resembling an antique water bottle. There is no pulmonary vascular congestion. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the radiographic findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dilated cardiomyopathy with biventricular failure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy with outflow obstruction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pericardial effusion with fluid accumulating within the pericardial sac

choices.label: D
choices.text: Right ventricular hypertrophy from chronic pulmonary hypertension

choices.label: E
choices.text: Left ventricular aneurysm following transmural myocardial infarction

explanation.summary: A globular, symmetrical, 'water bottle' cardiac silhouette on chest X-ray is characteristic of a pericardial effusion, distinguished from cardiomegaly which produces an asymmetric silhouette.
explanation.whyCorrect: A slowly enlarging pericardial effusion produces a rounded, symmetrical (globular) cardiac silhouette known as a 'water bottle' heart due to the attachment of the pericardial sac to the proximal ascending aorta. The absence of pulmonary congestion further supports effusion over cardiomyopathy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Dilated cardiomyopathy causes cardiomegaly with an asymmetric silhouette and is typically accompanied by signs of pulmonary vascular congestion, unlike the symmetrical globular shape described here.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy involves myocardial wall thickening that does not typically produce a significantly enlarged or globular cardiac silhouette on chest radiograph.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Right ventricular hypertrophy causes an asymmetric cardiac enlargement with prominence of the right heart border, not a symmetrical water bottle configuration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A left ventricular aneurysm produces a focal bulge along the left heart border rather than the diffuse, symmetrical globular enlargement described.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with a history of coronary artery disease is evaluated for recurrent exertional chest pain. His cardiologist orders an imaging study in which a radioactive tracer is injected and images are obtained using a specialized radiation-detecting camera after both rest and exercise. Images reveal decreased perfusion of the cardiac apex during the stress phase that normalizes at rest. Which of the following imaging modalities was most likely performed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coronary artery calcium scoring by non-contrast CT

choices.label: B
choices.text: Conventional coronary angiography with intraarterial dye

choices.label: C
choices.text: CT angiography with intravenous iodinated contrast

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nuclear stress testing with SPECT or PET imaging

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stress echocardiography with dobutamine infusion

explanation.summary: Nuclear stress testing uses injected radiopharmaceutical tracers and specialized radiation-detecting cameras (SPECT or PET) to evaluate myocardial perfusion at rest and during stress.
explanation.whyCorrect: The description matches nuclear stress testing: a radioactive tracer is injected, and images are obtained using specialized radiation-detecting cameras (SPECT or PET) to compare myocardial perfusion during stress versus rest. Decreased apical perfusion during stress corresponds to the distal LAD territory.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Coronary artery calcium scoring uses a low-dose non-contrast CT to measure calcium deposits and provides a numerical score but does not use radioactive tracers or detect myocardial perfusion.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Conventional coronary angiography uses an intraarterial catheter with radiopaque dye to directly visualize coronary arteries but does not use radioactive tracers or radiation-detecting cameras.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CT angiography is a noninvasive test using intravenous contrast to visualize coronary anatomy but does not employ radioactive tracers or SPECT/PET cameras.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Stress echocardiography uses ultrasound to assess wall motion abnormalities during stress but does not involve radioactive tracer injection or radiation-detecting cameras.

questions.stem: A 69-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents for a routine health maintenance visit. He has no abdominal symptoms. His physician recommends a screening imaging study for abdominal aortic aneurysm. Which of the following is the most appropriate screening modality for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: CT angiography of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast

choices.label: B
choices.text: Magnetic resonance angiography of the abdominal aorta

choices.label: C
choices.text: Conventional angiography with femoral artery catheterization

choices.label: D
choices.text: Non-contrast CT of the abdomen with 3D reconstruction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Abdominal ultrasound to measure aortic diameter

explanation.summary: Ultrasound is the study of choice for screening for abdominal aortic aneurysms. Screening is recommended for men aged 65-75 with a history of smoking or family history of aneurysms.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ultrasound is the recommended screening test for AAA because it is noninvasive, safe, and effective at measuring aortic diameter. This patient meets screening criteria as a male aged 65-75 with a significant smoking history.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CT angiography provides excellent aortic imaging but involves ionizing radiation and contrast, making it inappropriate as a first-line screening tool in an asymptomatic patient.
whyWrongByChoice.B: MR angiography provides detailed vascular imaging but is more costly and time-consuming than ultrasound and is not used as the initial screening modality for AAA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Conventional angiography is an invasive procedure requiring arterial catheterization and is not appropriate for screening an asymptomatic patient for AAA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Non-contrast CT of the abdomen can detect aneurysms but involves ionizing radiation and is not the recommended first-line screening test when ultrasound is available.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with stable exertional angina undergoes a stress echocardiogram. During peak exercise, imaging reveals hypokinesis of the anterolateral and anterior septal walls with preserved contractility of other segments. Which of the following coronary arteries is most likely responsible for the perfusion deficit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Right coronary artery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left circumflex artery

choices.label: C
choices.text: Left anterior descending artery

choices.label: D
choices.text: Posterior descending artery

choices.label: E
choices.text: Left main coronary artery

explanation.summary: Anterolateral and anterior septal regional wall motion abnormalities correspond to the left anterior descending artery territory.
explanation.whyCorrect: Regional wall motion abnormality (RWMA) in the anterolateral and anterior septal walls corresponds to the Left Anterior Descending Artery (LAD) territory. RWMA is the earliest clinical sign in the cardiac ischemic cascade, occurring even before anginal symptoms or ECG changes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The right coronary artery supplies the inferior wall and portions of the posterior wall; its occlusion would produce inferior wall motion abnormalities, not anterolateral and septal deficits.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The left circumflex artery supplies the lateral and posterolateral walls; its occlusion would not produce the anterolateral and anterior septal pattern described here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The posterior descending artery supplies the inferior interventricular septum and inferior wall; its territory does not correspond to anterolateral and anterior septal segments.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Left main coronary artery disease would produce widespread wall motion abnormalities affecting both LAD and circumflex territories, not the isolated anterolateral and anterior septal pattern described.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department with acute chest pain and hypotension. Bedside echocardiography reveals anechoic fluid surrounding the heart with collapse of the right ventricle during diastole. Her blood pressure is 78/50 mmHg and jugular venous pressure is elevated. Which of the following best explains the hemodynamic compromise in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased left ventricular contractility from acute myocarditis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Intrapericardial pressure exceeding right-sided chamber pressures and impairing venous return

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acute mitral valve rupture causing severe regurgitation and pulmonary edema

choices.label: D
choices.text: Massive pulmonary embolism causing acute right ventricular pressure overload

choices.label: E
choices.text: Acute aortic dissection extending into the coronary ostia with myocardial infarction

explanation.summary: Anechoic pericardial fluid with right ventricular collapse indicates cardiac tamponade, where intrapericardial pressure exceeds right-sided chamber pressures, impairing venous return and reducing cardiac output.
explanation.whyCorrect: A rapidly accumulating pericardial effusion causes cardiac tamponade by increasing intrapericardial pressure above right-sided chamber pressures. This impairs venous return, decreasing cardiac output and causing hypotension. Right ventricular or atrial collapse is seen on echo because right-sided pressures are lower than left-sided pressures.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Acute myocarditis would show ventricular dilation and diffuse hypokinesis on echocardiography rather than anechoic pericardial fluid with right ventricular collapse.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Acute mitral valve rupture would present with a flail leaflet and severe regurgitation jet on echo, along with pulmonary edema, not anechoic pericardial fluid with chamber collapse.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Massive pulmonary embolism causes right ventricular dilation and pressure overload but does not produce the anechoic pericardial fluid collection with right ventricular diastolic collapse seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While aortic dissection can cause tamponade via hemopericardium, the mechanism of hemodynamic compromise in this case is specifically the elevated intrapericardial pressure exceeding right-sided pressures and impairing venous return.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with a history of hypertension presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe tearing chest pain radiating to his back. His blood pressure is 182/98 mmHg in the right arm and 140/82 mmHg in the left arm. ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy without ST changes. A chest radiograph demonstrates mediastinal widening. Which of the following is the most likely underlying pathological process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Germ cell tumor arising from ectopic pluripotent stem cells in the anterior mediastinum

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rupture of atherosclerotic plaque within a coronary artery causing acute thrombosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Tear in the intimal lining of the aorta with blood dissecting between intima and media

choices.label: D
choices.text: Shearing force at the aortic isthmus from sudden deceleration injury

choices.label: E
choices.text: Malignant lymphatic proliferation causing bulky mediastinal lymphadenopathy

explanation.summary: Sudden severe tearing chest pain radiating to the back with unequal arm blood pressures and mediastinal widening in a hypertensive patient is the classic presentation of thoracic aortic dissection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Thoracic aortic dissection involves a tear in the intimal lining of the aorta, allowing blood to dissect between the intima and media. It occurs as a complication of chronic hypertension, connective tissue disorders, or atherosclerosis. The unequal arm pressures indicate the dissection flap is occluding an aortic branch.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A mediastinal teratoma is typically a slow-growing mass that does not present with acute tearing chest pain, blood pressure discrepancy between arms, or sudden onset symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Acute coronary syndrome from plaque rupture presents with chest pain but not tearing quality radiating to the back, blood pressure discrepancies between arms, or mediastinal widening.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Traumatic aortic disruption occurs after major deceleration injuries such as motor vehicle collisions, not spontaneously in a patient presenting from home with hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lymphoma causing mediastinal widening is a gradual process that does not present with acute tearing chest pain or blood pressure discrepancies between arms.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with known coronary artery disease has a CT angiography of the coronary arteries that reveals a high-grade stenosis of the left anterior descending artery. His cardiologist recommends a procedure that can both directly visualize the stenosis and provide definitive treatment in the same session. Which of the following is the most appropriate procedure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Nuclear medicine myocardial perfusion imaging with pharmacologic stress

choices.label: B
choices.text: Repeat CT angiography with 3D reconstruction for surgical planning

choices.label: C
choices.text: Conventional coronary angiography with percutaneous coronary intervention

choices.label: D
choices.text: Coronary artery calcium scoring to quantify overall disease burden

choices.label: E
choices.text: Stress echocardiography with exercise treadmill protocol

explanation.summary: Conventional coronary angiography (CCA) allows both direct visualization of coronary stenosis and therapeutic intervention via percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) with balloon dilation, stenting, or atherectomy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Conventional coronary angiography uses an intraarterial catheter advanced through the femoral or radial artery to inject dye and directly visualize the coronary arteries. The major advantage of CCA over noninvasive techniques is the ability to perform percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) during the same procedure, using balloons, stents, or rotational devices to treat stenosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Nuclear medicine stress testing assesses myocardial perfusion but does not directly visualize coronary anatomy or provide any therapeutic intervention for stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: CT angiography is a noninvasive diagnostic study that can identify stenosis but cannot provide treatment such as balloon dilation or stenting during the procedure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Coronary artery calcium scoring provides a numerical risk assessment score but does not use contrast, does not visualize coronary stenosis, and has no therapeutic capability.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Stress echocardiography evaluates wall motion abnormalities and cardiac function under stress but cannot directly visualize coronary arteries or provide therapeutic intervention.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man presents with acute decompensation of chronic heart failure. His chest radiograph shows cardiomegaly and bilateral airspace opacification that is most dense centrally around the hilar vessels and diminishes in intensity toward the peripheral lung fields. This pattern of opacification is best described by which of the following terms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cephalization of pulmonary blood flow

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bronchial cuffing with peribronchial thickening

choices.label: C
choices.text: Batwinging pattern of pulmonary edema

choices.label: D
choices.text: Costophrenic angle blunting from pleural fluid

choices.label: E
choices.text: Kerley B lines from interlobular septal thickening

explanation.summary: The batwinging pattern describes pulmonary edema that radiates outward from the hilar vessels, with the most peripheral lung fields being less affected.
explanation.whyCorrect: In cardiogenic pulmonary edema, airspace opacification due to alveolar fluid begins centrally surrounding the hilar vessels where pulmonary capillary pressure is highest, then propagates laterally with the most peripheral lung fields often less affected. This perihilar distribution radiating outward is called the batwinging pattern.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cephalization of flow refers specifically to increased visibility and size of the apical pulmonary vessels due to elevated pulmonary artery pressures, not the described central-to-peripheral airspace opacification pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Bronchial cuffing refers to a silhouette of edema fluid around small radiolucent bronchi, which is a focal peribronchial finding rather than the described pattern of central-dominant bilateral airspace opacification.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Costophrenic angle blunting indicates pleural effusion, which is fluid between the visceral and parietal pleura, not the described airspace opacification pattern radiating from the hila.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Kerley B lines are short, thin, linear peripheral opacities representing interlobular septal thickening from interstitial edema, not the broad central airspace opacification described.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with Turner syndrome is referred for cardiovascular screening. Her physician is concerned about the risk of thoracic aortic aneurysm and orders an imaging study. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial screening modality for thoracic aortic aneurysm in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Abdominal ultrasound with aortic diameter measurement

choices.label: B
choices.text: CT angiography of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Conventional aortography via femoral catheterization

choices.label: D
choices.text: Coronary artery calcium scoring by low-dose CT

choices.label: E
choices.text: Echocardiography to visualize the thoracic aorta

explanation.summary: Screening for thoracic aortic aneurysms in patients with Turner syndrome or connective tissue disorders is performed using echocardiography.
explanation.whyCorrect: Screening for thoracic aortic aneurysms is recommended for individuals with Turner syndrome or connective tissue disorders. This screening is performed using echocardiography, which can visualize the thoracic aorta and aortic root noninvasively.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Abdominal ultrasound is the screening study of choice for abdominal aortic aneurysms in men aged 65-75 with smoking history, not for thoracic aortic aneurysms in Turner syndrome patients.
whyWrongByChoice.B: CT angiography provides excellent aortic imaging but involves radiation and contrast; it is used for detailed evaluation after screening rather than as the initial screening modality for thoracic aortic aneurysm.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Conventional aortography is an invasive procedure requiring arterial catheterization and is not appropriate as a screening tool for asymptomatic patients.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Coronary artery calcium scoring measures coronary plaque calcium deposits for cardiac risk assessment and does not evaluate the thoracic aorta for aneurysmal disease.
